questions.stem: A full-term baby boy is born by elective Caesarean section. He develops signs of respiratory distress at 30 minutes of age.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: TTN (wet lung) is the classic post-elective C-section diagnosis. Fluid resorption from the lungs is impaired because labour-related catecholamines that drive fluid clearance are absent. Presents within 2 hours, resolves by 48–72 hours.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Prostaglandin E1 (alprostadil) keeps the ductus arteriosus patent — this is the hallmark treatment of duct-dependent congenital heart disease. TGA creates two parallel, non-mixing circuits; the duct is the only mixing point.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Neonatal lupus erythematosus — maternal anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies cross the placenta and damage the fetal AV node causing congenital complete heart block. May require pacemaker.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Neonatal Abstinence Syndrome (NAS): onset 48–72 h for opioids. Finnegan score used to monitor severity. Classic triad: CNS excitability + GI dysfunction + autonomic instability (sneezing, yawning, sweating).

questions.keyPoint: TTN vs RDS: TTN is a TERM/late-preterm baby, post C-section, quick onset, resolves in 48 h. RDS is PRETERM (<34 weeks), ground-glass CXR, needs surfactant.

questions.stem: A 3-day-old baby who was born at 30 weeks' gestation shows no signs of respiratory distress, but has recurrent pauses in his breathing that last for more than 20 seconds.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Patent ductus arteriosus is treated with indomethacin (or ibuprofen) — COX inhibitors reduce prostaglandin E2, causing ductal closure. Side-effect: renal vasoconstriction → oliguria, electrolyte disturbances.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Chronic lung disease (bronchopulmonary dysplasia) = oxygen dependency >36 weeks corrected gestational age. CXR: sponge/cystic appearance, hyperinflation. Results from barotrauma + oxygen toxicity.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Retinopathy of prematurity (ROP) — hyperoxia damages the developing retinal vasculature causing neovascularisation. Screened at 4–6 weeks post-birth in infants <32 weeks or <1500 g.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Apnoea of prematurity: pauses ≥20 seconds (or shorter if accompanied by bradycardia/desaturation). Due to immature central respiratory drive. Caffeine citrate is first-line treatment.

questions.keyPoint: NEC triad to remember: premature + formula-fed + 2nd week of life. Bell staging guides management — Stage I = monitor, Stage III = surgery.

choices.label: I

questions.stem: A baby girl is born at 35 weeks' gestation by Caesarean section. She develops jaundice on day 2 with a maximum bilirubin level of 200 mmol/l on day 3. The jaundice resolves by day 7.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physiological jaundice: onset day 2–3, peaks day 3–5, resolves by day 7–10 (term) or day 14 (preterm). Due to high RBC turnover, immature UDP-glucuronosyltransferase, and increased enterohepatic circulation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy): unconjugated bilirubin crosses BBB → deposits in basal ganglia/hippocampus. Acute: irritability, hypotonia then hypertonia, opisthotonus, high-pitched cry. Chronic: choreoathetoid CP, deafness, dental enamel dysplasia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: G6PD deficiency: X-linked recessive, prevalent in Mediterranean/Middle Eastern/African populations. Triggers: infection, certain drugs (antimalarials, sulfonamides), naphthalene. Presents with haemolytic jaundice days-to-weeks after discharge.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: ABO incompatibility: mother group O (natural anti-A, anti-B IgG antibodies crossing placenta) vs baby group A or B. Jaundice within 24 hours (pathological). Unlike Rh disease, can occur in first pregnancy.

questions.keyPoint: Jaundice in first 24 hours is ALWAYS pathological. The 3 most common causes: Rh incompatibility, ABO incompatibility, G6PD deficiency. Use the 'exchange transfusion' threshold charts — phototherapy first, then exchange if rising despite treatment.

questions.stem: A baby boy is born by emergency Caesarean section at 37 weeks' gestation due to rising maternal anti-D titres and pleural effusions seen on the baby's antenatal scan. He is very oedematous at delivery, and the cord bilirubin level is 50 mmol/l.

choices.label: B

questions.stem: A baby boy was born at term by normal vaginal delivery, and had a birth weight of 4.9 kg. Neonatal examination revealed an abnormal Moro reflex. The Moro reflex was normal on the left side, but there was very little movement of the right arm. There was positive hand grasp bilaterally.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Erb's palsy: C5–C6 injury (upper brachial plexus) — large baby, shoulder dystocia. Classic 'waiter's tip' position. Hand grasp PRESERVED (C8-T1 intact). Moro absent on affected side. Klumpke = C8-T1 (hand weak, grasp lost).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Congenital cataract: absent/white red reflex. Causes: TORCH infections, galactosaemia, Down syndrome, idiopathic. Must be treated urgently (weeks) to prevent irreversible amblyopia.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Turner's syndrome (45X0): females, puffy feet (lymphoedema) at birth, webbed neck/cystic hygroma, wide-carrying angle, short stature, primary amenorrhoea. Coarctation of aorta in 15–30%.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Cleft palate: unable to create negative pressure for suckling despite hunger. Nasal regurgitation (milk coming from nostrils) is pathognomonic. May be isolated or part of Pierre Robin sequence.

questions.keyPoint: Erb's vs Klumpke memory hack — 'Erb = Upper (C5-C6) = waiter's tip, hand works'. 'Klumpke = Lower (C8-T1) = hand/claw affected, possible Horner syndrome'.

questions.stem: A baby girl who was born at term is found to have puffy feet and a cystic hygroma at her neonatal examination.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis: meconium ileus occurs in 10–15% of CF neonates. Inspissated meconium obstructs the distal ileum → failure to pass meconium >48 h, bilious vomiting, distension. No anorectal malformation visible (distinguishes from imperforate anus).

questions.keyPoint: Erb's vs Klumpke memory hack — 'Erb = Upper (C5-C6) = waiter's tip, hand works'. 'Klumpke = Lower (C8-T1) = hand/claw affected, possible Horner syndrome'.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old, who had a difficult delivery, has been observed by nursery staff to be much clumsier than the other children. She also finds it difficult to copy shapes.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebral palsy: non-progressive motor disorder due to perinatal brain injury. History of difficult delivery is a red flag. Clumsiness and fine motor difficulties suggest dyskinetic/ataxic CP. Copying shapes is a fine motor/perceptual task.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Autism Spectrum Disorder: triad of impaired social interaction + communication difficulties + restricted/repetitive behaviours. The insistence on taking the toy train everywhere = rigidity/resistance to change.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile convulsion (otitis media as underlying fever source — most common cause of febrile seizure in this age): tonic-clonic, age 6 months–6 years, associated with fever, duration <15 min. Otitis media is the most common infection triggering febrile seizures.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Non-paralytic strabismus (comitant squint): the squint angle remains constant in all directions of gaze — this means full range of movement. Paralytic (incomitant) strabismus has limited range due to CN III, IV, or VI palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Autism vs Asperger's: In Asperger's, language and cognitive development are NORMAL — they're often highly verbal with good vocabulary. The social deficits and restricted interests are present but without the language delay seen in autism.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old has presented to the GP surgery with a squint. There is a full range of movement in both eyes.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebral palsy: non-progressive motor disorder due to perinatal brain injury. History of difficult delivery is a red flag. Clumsiness and fine motor difficulties suggest dyskinetic/ataxic CP. Copying shapes is a fine motor/perceptual task.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Autism Spectrum Disorder: triad of impaired social interaction + communication difficulties + restricted/repetitive behaviours. The insistence on taking the toy train everywhere = rigidity/resistance to change.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile convulsion (otitis media as underlying fever source — most common cause of febrile seizure in this age): tonic-clonic, age 6 months–6 years, associated with fever, duration <15 min. Otitis media is the most common infection triggering febrile seizures.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Non-paralytic strabismus (comitant squint): the squint angle remains constant in all directions of gaze — this means full range of movement. Paralytic (incomitant) strabismus has limited range due to CN III, IV, or VI palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Autism vs Asperger's: In Asperger's, language and cognitive development are NORMAL — they're often highly verbal with good vocabulary. The social deficits and restricted interests are present but without the language delay seen in autism.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy has severely delayed speech and poor social interaction. He is obsessed with trains and will not go anywhere unless he has his toy train with him.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebral palsy: non-progressive motor disorder due to perinatal brain injury. History of difficult delivery is a red flag. Clumsiness and fine motor difficulties suggest dyskinetic/ataxic CP. Copying shapes is a fine motor/perceptual task.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Autism Spectrum Disorder: triad of impaired social interaction + communication difficulties + restricted/repetitive behaviours. The insistence on taking the toy train everywhere = rigidity/resistance to change.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile convulsion (otitis media as underlying fever source — most common cause of febrile seizure in this age): tonic-clonic, age 6 months–6 years, associated with fever, duration <15 min. Otitis media is the most common infection triggering febrile seizures.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Non-paralytic strabismus (comitant squint): the squint angle remains constant in all directions of gaze — this means full range of movement. Paralytic (incomitant) strabismus has limited range due to CN III, IV, or VI palsy.

questions.keyPoint: Autism vs Asperger's: In Asperger's, language and cognitive development are NORMAL — they're often highly verbal with good vocabulary. The social deficits and restricted interests are present but without the language delay seen in autism.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl is admitted with a high fever, irritability and a seizure. A lumbar puncture confirms meningitis. After she has been treated and discharged, nursery staff notice that she is more disruptive and she does not do what she is told.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cerebral palsy: non-progressive motor disorder due to perinatal brain injury. History of difficult delivery is a red flag. Clumsiness and fine motor difficulties suggest dyskinetic/ataxic CP. Copying shapes is a fine motor/perceptual task.

choices.label: C

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Sensorineural deafness as a complication of meningitis (bacterial meningitis → cochlear inflammation/damage). Post-meningitis hearing loss causes apparent behavioural change — the child is 'not listening' because she cannot hear.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl presents with a paroxysmal, unilateral headache accompanied by nausea.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Migraine: unilateral (can be bilateral in children), throbbing, associated with nausea/vomiting, photophobia/phonophobia, lasts 2–72 hours. Paroxysmal = episodic attacks with symptom-free intervals.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Temporo-mandibular joint (TMJ) dysfunction: headache or facial pain exacerbated by chewing, jaw movement, or clenching. Often associated with dental malocclusion, bruxism, or jaw trauma.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Craniopharyngioma: suprasellar tumour. RAISED ICP features — morning headache worse on lying (CSF stasis), faltering growth (GH deficiency), visual field defects (bitemporal hemianopia). This progressive pattern with postural worsening is a red flag for SOL.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Tension-type headache: bilateral, 'band-like' or pressure sensation, no nausea/vomiting, no photophobia, non-pulsating. Chronic daily headache pattern is typical in adolescents under academic/social stress.

questions.keyPoint: Red flags in paediatric headache (SNNOOP10): Systemic illness/fever, Neurological signs, Onset sudden ('thunderclap'), Older age, Progressive pattern, Postural change, Papilloedema, Prior headache history change. Morning headache worse supine = raised ICP until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: A child can build a tower of six bricks.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Crawling = 9 months. Sitting unsupported = 6–7 months. Pulling to stand = 9–10 months. Cruising = 10–11 months.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: First words (3–5 meaningful words) = 12 months. By 18 months: 6–20 words. By 2 years: 50+ words and 2-word combinations.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Tower of 6 bricks = 2 years. Memory hack: number of bricks × 3 = age in months up to ~18 months, then: 18M=3 bricks, 2Y=6 bricks, 3Y=9 bricks.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Drawing shapes: Circle = 3 years, Cross = 4 years, Square = 4–5 years, Triangle = 5 years. Mnemonic: CiCle=3, Cross=4, Square=5.

questions.keyPoint: Quick milestone memory: 6W=smile, 3M=head control, 6M=sit with support, 9M=pincer grip starts, 12M=walk+words, 18M=6 words, 2Y=50 words+2-word sentences, 3Y=copy circle+strangers understand speech.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old girl presents with episodes of abnormal movements involving the neck, face and extremities, associated with inappropriate speech, which last for about 10 seconds. She complains of 'feeling funny' just before an episode.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Infantile spasms (West syndrome): onset 3–12 months, sudden flexion/extension spasms in clusters, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, developmental regression. Associated with tuberous sclerosis (25%), Down syndrome, hypoxic injury.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sturge-Weber syndrome: port-wine stain (facial capillary haemangioma, usually V1/V2 trigeminal distribution) + ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma → contralateral seizures, hemiplegia, intellectual disability. Port-wine stain = right face → left-sided seizures.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Simple partial seizure: consciousness PRESERVED. Focal motor, sensory, or autonomic symptoms. Aura ('feeling funny') = beginning of partial seizure. No postictal confusion. Duration seconds to minutes.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME): adolescence onset, early morning myoclonic jerks (especially on waking), often associated with generalised tonic-clonic seizures triggered by sleep deprivation. 'Brushing teeth = early morning = JME'.

questions.keyPoint: Phacomatoses (neurocutaneous syndromes): Tuberous sclerosis = Ash-leaf macules + Shagreen patches + Infantile spasms. Sturge-Weber = Port-wine stain + contralateral seizures. NF1 = Café-au-lait spots + Lisch nodules. VHL = haemangioblastomas.

questions.stem: A 6-month-old boy presents with brief sudden contractions that result in flexion of the body. The attacks occur in clusters. He also appears to have developmental regression.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Infantile spasms (West syndrome): onset 3–12 months, sudden flexion/extension spasms in clusters, hypsarrhythmia on EEG, developmental regression. Associated with tuberous sclerosis (25%), Down syndrome, hypoxic injury.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Sturge-Weber syndrome: port-wine stain (facial capillary haemangioma, usually V1/V2 trigeminal distribution) + ipsilateral leptomeningeal angioma → contralateral seizures, hemiplegia, intellectual disability. Port-wine stain = right face → left-sided seizures.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Simple partial seizure: consciousness PRESERVED. Focal motor, sensory, or autonomic symptoms. Aura ('feeling funny') = beginning of partial seizure. No postictal confusion. Duration seconds to minutes.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Reflex anoxic seizures: triggered by pain/fright → vagal bradycardia/asystole → cerebral hypoperfusion → pallor + LOC + twitching. Distinguished from breath-holding attacks (cyanotic — child holds breath then faints) by pallor and vagal trigger.

questions.keyPoint: Phacomatoses (neurocutaneous syndromes): Tuberous sclerosis = Ash-leaf macules + Shagreen patches + Infantile spasms. Sturge-Weber = Port-wine stain + contralateral seizures. NF1 = Café-au-lait spots + Lisch nodules. VHL = haemangioblastomas.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl develops a fever and stops moving her right arm. On examination she is found to have a swollen hot elbow. Movement of the joint is limited by pain.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Scarlet fever (Group A Streptococcus): fever + sore throat + strawberry tongue + fine sandpaper-like papular rash (blanches, starts trunk, spares palms). Notifiable disease. Treat with amoxicillin/penicillin V.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum: most dangerous malaria species, Sub-Saharan Africa. Fever + rigors post-travel to endemic area. Falciparum causes cerebral malaria, severe anaemia, renal failure. Thick and thin blood films diagnosis.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: TB: classic B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) >2 weeks + hilar lymphadenopathy. Primary TB in children. Mantoux/IGRA testing, sputum/gastric washings for AFB. Contact tracing essential.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Septic arthritis: Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause in children >2 years. Hot, swollen, exquisitely tender joint with pseudoparalysis. Emergency: delay causes avascular necrosis. IV antibiotics + joint washout.

questions.keyPoint: Septic arthritis vs transient synovitis (Kocher criteria): fever >38.5°C, inability to weight-bear, ESR >40, WCC >12,000. 4 criteria = 99% probability septic arthritis.

questions.stem: The parents of a 2-year-old girl are worried because she has had a cold for the past 3 days, and she is now very hoarse and finds it difficult to breathe because of her barking cough, which is worse at night. On examination you notice intermittent stridor. The child does not look toxic and her immunisations are up to date.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Scarlet fever (Group A Streptococcus): fever + sore throat + strawberry tongue + fine sandpaper-like papular rash (blanches, starts trunk, spares palms). Notifiable disease. Treat with amoxicillin/penicillin V.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis): Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3) is the most common cause. Barking/seal cough, hoarse voice, stridor worse at night, no toxicity. Steeple sign on CXR. Dexamethasone/budesonide treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Septic arthritis vs transient synovitis (Kocher criteria): fever >38.5°C, inability to weight-bear, ESR >40, WCC >12,000. 4 criteria = 99% probability septic arthritis.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with a 3-day history of fever and sore throat. On examination she is found to have a red tongue and a generalised papular rash.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Scarlet fever (Group A Streptococcus): fever + sore throat + strawberry tongue + fine sandpaper-like papular rash (blanches, starts trunk, spares palms). Notifiable disease. Treat with amoxicillin/penicillin V.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum: most dangerous malaria species, Sub-Saharan Africa. Fever + rigors post-travel to endemic area. Falciparum causes cerebral malaria, severe anaemia, renal failure. Thick and thin blood films diagnosis.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: TB: classic B-symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss) >2 weeks + hilar lymphadenopathy. Primary TB in children. Mantoux/IGRA testing, sputum/gastric washings for AFB. Contact tracing essential.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Croup (laryngotracheobronchitis): Parainfluenza virus (types 1 and 3) is the most common cause. Barking/seal cough, hoarse voice, stridor worse at night, no toxicity. Steeple sign on CXR. Dexamethasone/budesonide treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Septic arthritis vs transient synovitis (Kocher criteria): fever >38.5°C, inability to weight-bear, ESR >40, WCC >12,000. 4 criteria = 99% probability septic arthritis.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy presents with a 3-day history of a cough, coryzal symptoms and conjunctivitis. He then develops a high fever and a blotchy red maculo-papular rash which starts behind his ears and spreads to his face and then to the rest of his body.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Infectious mononucleosis (EBV): ampicillin/amoxicillin rash is classic — 90% develop maculopapular rash if given aminopenicillins. Jaundice indicates hepatitis (common in EBV). Atypical lymphocytes, heterophile antibody (Monospot) positive.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Measles: prodrome of cough + coryza + conjunctivitis (3 C's) + Koplik spots → rash starts retroauricular spreading cephalocaudally. Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa) are pathognomonic. Notifiable disease.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fifth disease (Erythema infectiosum, Parvovirus B19): 'slapped cheek' appearance. Mild prodrome then bright red facial rash. Once rash appears, child is no longer infectious. Risk in pregnancy: hydrops fetalis.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Meningococcal sepsis: petechial/purpuric non-blanching rash + fever + shock = medical emergency. Neisseria meningitidis. IM benzylpenicillin immediately (in community before hospital transfer). Mortality 5–10% even with treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Rash timing post-fever for exanthems: Roseola (HHV-6) = rash AFTER fever breaks. Measles = rash WITH high fever. Fifth disease = rash after mild prodrome. Chickenpox = rash WITH fever. Scarlet fever = rash DURING fever.

choices.label: A

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Varicella zoster (chickenpox): centripetal distribution (face/trunk first), vesicular 'dewdrop on rose petal', crops at different stages simultaneously, extremely pruritic. Ataxia = cerebellar involvement (uncommon complication).

questions.stem: A 2-month-old girl presents with a history of recurrent and atypical chest infections. Examination reveals severe wasting of the quadriceps and buttocks, with evidence of candidiasis in her mouth. Blood tests show undetectable levels of T cells, NK cells and immunoglobulins.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Bacterial (spontaneous bacterial) peritonitis complicating nephrotic syndrome: low albumin = ascites + oedema + protein loss. Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis occurs in nephrotic syndrome due to impaired opsonisation (loss of immunoglobulins in urine). Streptococcus pneumoniae most common.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Still's disease (systemic JIA): quotidian (daily spiking) fever + salmon-pink evanescent rash (appears with fever spikes) + arthritis + organomegaly + lymphadenopathy. High ferritin is characteristic. Complication: macrophage activation syndrome.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Cow's milk protein intolerance (CMPI): villous atrophy without coeliac-specific antibodies (because gluten not yet introduced — exclusively formula-fed). Recurrent infections suggest secondary immunodeficiency from protein-losing enteropathy. Resolves on hydrolysed/amino acid formula.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: SCID: absent T+B+NK cells (all lymphocyte lineages). Presents in first months of life with failure to thrive, recurrent opportunistic infections, oral candidiasis. Fatal without HSCT. Includes ADA deficiency, RAG1/2 mutations, common gamma chain deficiency.

questions.keyPoint: Still's disease triad: Quotidian fever + Salmon rash + Arthritis. The rash is evanescent — it comes and goes WITH the fever. Ferritin >10,000 suggests MAS (macrophage activation syndrome), a life-threatening complication.

questions.stem: Children with an allergy to eggs should be given this vaccine under hospital supervision.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hib vaccine: Haemophilus influenzae type B was the most common cause of acute epiglottitis and bacterial meningitis in children. Since Hib vaccination introduction, epiglottitis is now rare.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: MMR (Measles, Mumps, Rubella): live attenuated vaccine given at 12–13 months (first dose) and pre-school booster at 3 years 4 months. Contraindicated in immunocompromised children.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: HPV vaccine: offered to girls (and boys in updated programmes) aged 12–13 years (Year 8) via school programmes. Protects against HPV types 16, 18 (cervical cancer), 6, 11 (genital warts) with the 4-valent Gardasil.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: MMR can be given to egg-allergic children in community — guidelines changed. The one that requires hospital supervision for severe egg allergy is Yellow fever (and some influenza vaccines). 'None of the above' because MMR is now safe in egg allergy.

questions.keyPoint: UK immunisation schedule key ages: 8/12/16 weeks = 6-in-1+MenB+PCV; 1 year = Hib/MenC+PCV+MMR+MenB; 3y4m = MMR booster+4-in-1; 12–13y = HPV; 14y = 3-in-1+MenACWY.

questions.stem: Introduction of this vaccine has led to a 99% decrease in the incidence of acute epiglottitis.

choices.label: C

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Cerebral palsy is NOT a contraindication to any standard UK immunisation. 'None of the above' is the correct answer — cerebral palsy children should receive all routine vaccinations.

questions.stem: A 2-year-old girl who was born at term was subsequently diagnosed with infantile polycystic kidney disease. Her height has decreased from the 50th percentile at 6 months of age to below the 0.4th percentile currently.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Renal failure (chronic kidney disease) causes growth failure due to: uraemia suppressing GH axis, renal osteodystrophy, metabolic acidosis, and poor nutrition. Progressive crossing of centiles in a child with known renal disease = renal failure.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: West syndrome (infantile spasms) is treated with ACTH (corticotropin) or prednisolone. Both cause Cushing-like syndrome including growth suppression (cortisol excess inhibits IGF-1 and direct effects on growth plates).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Kwashiorkor: protein-deficient malnutrition. Classic features: oedema (from hypoalbuminaemia), skin changes (flaky paint dermatitis), hair changes (flag sign), moon face, hepatomegaly. Distinguished from marasmus (pure caloric deficiency with wasting but NO oedema).

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Constitutional growth delay (CGD): normal parental height, delayed bone age (with preserved growth velocity), normal investigations. Hallmark: they eventually reach normal adult height — just late. Often family history of 'late developers'.

questions.keyPoint: Marasmus vs Kwashiorkor memory: 'Marasmus = Must Wasting' (wasted, no oedema, severe caloric deficiency). 'Kwashiorkor = Kwelling (swelling)' (oedematous, protein deficient). Both can coexist (marasmic-kwashiorkor).

questions.stem: A 4-year-old child is being investigated for short stature. She has missed several previous appointments and appears unkempt. Investigations are normal.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Renal failure (chronic kidney disease) causes growth failure due to: uraemia suppressing GH axis, renal osteodystrophy, metabolic acidosis, and poor nutrition. Progressive crossing of centiles in a child with known renal disease = renal failure.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: West syndrome (infantile spasms) is treated with ACTH (corticotropin) or prednisolone. Both cause Cushing-like syndrome including growth suppression (cortisol excess inhibits IGF-1 and direct effects on growth plates).

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Constitutional growth delay (CGD): normal parental height, delayed bone age (with preserved growth velocity), normal investigations. Hallmark: they eventually reach normal adult height — just late. Often family history of 'late developers'.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Deprivation dwarfism (psychosocial short stature): emotional neglect/deprivation causes functional hypopituitarism with low GH. Normal investigations because GH secretion normalises when removed from neglectful environment. Social red flags: missed appointments, unkempt appearance.

questions.keyPoint: Marasmus vs Kwashiorkor memory: 'Marasmus = Must Wasting' (wasted, no oedema, severe caloric deficiency). 'Kwashiorkor = Kwelling (swelling)' (oedematous, protein deficient). Both can coexist (marasmic-kwashiorkor).

questions.stem: A 2-month-old boy presents with a history of intermittent acute abdominal pain accompanied by screaming and pallor, followed by a period of quietness. He has a centrally palpable sausage-shaped mass, and his mother reports that there has been blood and mucus in his stools.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Reye's syndrome: aspirin use during viral illness → mitochondrial dysfunction → hepatic encephalopathy + hypoglycaemia + elevated LFTs/ammonia. This is why aspirin is CONTRAINDICATED in children <16 years.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Anal fissure: most common cause of rectal bleeding in children. Constipated stool → mucosal tear → painful defecation → child withholds → more constipation (vicious cycle). Treatment: stool softeners (lactulose/movicol), high fibre, topical GTN/diltiazem for chronic fissure.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Appendicitis: calcified faecolith on plain X-ray in ~10% of paediatric appendicitis — it is a useful sign when present. Combined with abdominal pain, low-grade fever, anorexia, right iliac fossa tenderness, this is appendicitis.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Intussusception: peak 3 months–2 years. Classic triad: colicky pain (screaming/pale periods) + sausage-shaped mass in RUQ + redcurrant jelly stools. Diagnosis: US (target sign). Treatment: air enema reduction (80% success) or surgery.

questions.keyPoint: Intussusception vs Meckel's: Both cause rectal bleeding in toddlers. Intussusception = PAINFUL (colicky) + sausage mass + redcurrant jelly. Meckel's = PAINLESS massive rectal bleeding + Tc-99m hot spot.

questions.topic: Poor growth — most likely test result
questions.optionsTitle: Poor growth — most likely test result
questions.stem: A 6-week-old boy, who has not regained his birth weight, presents with non-bilious projectile vomiting and dehydration. On examination, an olive-shaped mass is palpated in the upper abdomen.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-infectious lactose intolerance (transient): rotavirus or other gastroenteritis damages brush-border lactase → secondary lactase deficiency. Reducing substances in stool (Clinitest) positive for lactose/glucose. Resolves in 4–6 weeks on lactose-free formula.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU): autosomal recessive, phenylalanine hydroxylase deficiency → high phenylalanine → intellectual disability. Musty/mousy odour is pathognomonic. Hypopigmentation (tyrosine needed for melanin synthesis). Seizures in untreated cases.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Hypertrophic pyloric stenosis: projectile non-bilious vomiting + olive mass → hypochloraemic hypokalemic METABOLIC ALKALOSIS (HCl lost in vomit). Baby is hungry, vomits after every feed. US confirmatory. Treatment: Ramstedt pyloromyotomy.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis: positive sweat test (Cl >60 mmol/L) + Delta F508 most common CFTR mutation (70% of CF alleles). Failure to thrive + recurrent chest infections is the classic paediatric presentation. CF screen now on neonatal heel prick (IRT → DNA).

questions.keyPoint: Pyloric stenosis electrolytes: Low Na+, Low K+, Low Cl−, High HCO3−, High pH = Hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis. Paradoxical aciduria (kidneys conserve Na+ preferentially and excrete H+ to maintain volume).

questions.stem: A 2-year-old boy, who was born in India, arrived in the UK 2 weeks ago. He is very small for his age. He presents to the GP with a history of seizures. His skin appears hypopigmented and has a musty smell.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-infectious lactose intolerance (transient): rotavirus or other gastroenteritis damages brush-border lactase → secondary lactase deficiency. Reducing substances in stool (Clinitest) positive for lactose/glucose. Resolves in 4–6 weeks on lactose-free formula.

choices.label: G

choices.label: L

questions.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents with nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps and watery diarrhoea that began 2 hours after eating leftover takeaway for breakfast.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Rotavirus: most common cause of viral gastroenteritis in children worldwide, especially under 5. Watery diarrhoea + vomiting, self-limiting 3–8 days. Now included in UK immunisation schedule (oral, at 8 and 12 weeks).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Bacillus cereus: emetic form presents within 1–6 hours of eating reheated rice or starchy foods. Pre-formed heat-stable toxin. The short incubation (2 hours) distinguishes it from Salmonella (12–36 h) and E. coli.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Campylobacter jejuni: most common bacterial gastroenteritis in UK. Bloody diarrhoea, severe cramps, fever, myalgia. Poultry is main source. Complications: reactive arthritis, Guillain-Barré syndrome. Self-limiting; azithromycin if severe.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Giardia lamblia: protozoan, cysts on stool microscopy, foul-smelling fatty diarrhoea, bloating, cramps. Acquired via contaminated water abroad. Treat with metronidazole.

questions.keyPoint: Food poisoning timing helps identify cause: <6h = preformed toxin (Staph aureus, B. cereus emetic). 6–12h = B. cereus diarrhoeal or C. perfringens. 12–36h = Salmonella, Campylobacter. >72h = Giardia, parasites, Yersinia.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old girl who is fit and well has been found to have an ejection systolic murmur of uniform intensity and brief duration. This murmur is intensified by fever, excitement and exercise. It varies in quality when she is examined lying down compared with sitting up.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physiological (innocent) murmur: soft, systolic, short, no radiation, varies with position, intensified by high output states (fever, exercise, anaemia, excitement). No associated symptoms. No thrill. Normal ECG and CXR. Still's murmur is the most common type.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot: 4 features — VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH. 'Tet spells' (hypercyanotic) on crying/exertion. CXR: boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot). Hypercyanotic spells treated with knee-chest position + morphine + propranolol.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: VSD: most common congenital heart defect. Pansystolic murmur at LLSE + parasternal thrill + signs of heart failure (sweating, tachycardia, hepatomegaly) from left-to-right shunt increasing pulmonary blood flow. Symptoms emerge at 2–8 weeks as PVR falls.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta (duct-dependent): absent/weak femoral pulses + radio-femoral delay + BP higher in arms than legs. Neonates with severe coarctation present in shock when duct closes. Prostaglandin E1 to maintain ductal patency until surgical repair.

questions.keyPoint: VSD vs ASD murmur: VSD = pansystolic, loudest LLSE, with thrill. ASD = ejection systolic (pulmonary flow murmur) + fixed split S2 + right heart enlargement. ASD rarely causes HF in childhood — presents with recurrent chest infections and fatigue.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old boy is being investigated for faltering growth, breathlessness and sweating on feeding. On examination he is tachycardic and tachypnoeic, and he has a parasternal thrill and a loud pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal edge. He also has hepatomegaly.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physiological (innocent) murmur: soft, systolic, short, no radiation, varies with position, intensified by high output states (fever, exercise, anaemia, excitement). No associated symptoms. No thrill. Normal ECG and CXR. Still's murmur is the most common type.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot: 4 features — VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH. 'Tet spells' (hypercyanotic) on crying/exertion. CXR: boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot). Hypercyanotic spells treated with knee-chest position + morphine + propranolol.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN): high PVR causes R-to-L shunting through PDA/foramen ovale → cyanosis. Loud P2 (S2). Treatment: inhaled NO, high-frequency oscillation, ECMO. Associated with meconium aspiration, perinatal asphyxia.

questions.keyPoint: VSD vs ASD murmur: VSD = pansystolic, loudest LLSE, with thrill. ASD = ejection systolic (pulmonary flow murmur) + fixed split S2 + right heart enlargement. ASD rarely causes HF in childhood — presents with recurrent chest infections and fatigue.

questions.keyPoint: VSD vs ASD murmur: VSD = pansystolic, loudest LLSE, with thrill. ASD = ejection systolic (pulmonary flow murmur) + fixed split S2 + right heart enlargement. ASD rarely causes HF in childhood — presents with recurrent chest infections and fatigue.

questions.stem: A 3-month-old boy was brought to A&E when his parents noticed that he was becoming 'blue' on crying. Examination revealed a loud pansystolic murmur, and a chest radiograph showed a boot-shaped heart.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Physiological (innocent) murmur: soft, systolic, short, no radiation, varies with position, intensified by high output states (fever, exercise, anaemia, excitement). No associated symptoms. No thrill. Normal ECG and CXR. Still's murmur is the most common type.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot: 4 features — VSD + pulmonary stenosis + overriding aorta + RVH. 'Tet spells' (hypercyanotic) on crying/exertion. CXR: boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot). Hypercyanotic spells treated with knee-chest position + morphine + propranolol.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: VSD: most common congenital heart defect. Pansystolic murmur at LLSE + parasternal thrill + signs of heart failure (sweating, tachycardia, hepatomegaly) from left-to-right shunt increasing pulmonary blood flow. Symptoms emerge at 2–8 weeks as PVR falls.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Coarctation of the aorta (duct-dependent): absent/weak femoral pulses + radio-femoral delay + BP higher in arms than legs. Neonates with severe coarctation present in shock when duct closes. Prostaglandin E1 to maintain ductal patency until surgical repair.

questions.keyPoint: VSD vs ASD murmur: VSD = pansystolic, loudest LLSE, with thrill. ASD = ejection systolic (pulmonary flow murmur) + fixed split S2 + right heart enlargement. ASD rarely causes HF in childhood — presents with recurrent chest infections and fatigue.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl with a history of a previously corrected coarctation of the aorta presents with a history of recurrent fevers and a temperature of 39°C. Examination reveals that she has splinter haemorrhages of her nails and an erythematous rash on the palms of her hands.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Infective endocarditis: splinter haemorrhages + Janeway lesions (erythematous palm rash) + fever in a child with structural heart disease (corrected coarctation leaves residual abnormality). Duke criteria diagnosis. IV antibiotics (empirical: amoxicillin + gentamicin).

choices.label: G
choices.text: Immersion of the face in ice-cold water
choices.explanation: SVT (supraventricular tachycardia): HR 280 bpm in infant. First-line: vagal manoeuvres — in infants, ice-cold water immersion of face (diving reflex activates). Adults: Valsalva. If unstable: adenosine IV. If vagal fails: IV adenosine 0.1 mg/kg.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Apnoea is a recognised side-effect of prostaglandin E1/E2 therapy. When apnoea occurs with hypercapnoea in a duct-dependent cardiac patient on prostaglandin, artificial ventilation (intubation) is required to manage the respiratory depression safely.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Modified BT shunt: subclavian artery to pulmonary artery shunt to increase pulmonary blood flow in severely cyanotic ToF while awaiting definitive repair (total correction). Prostaglandin buys time but is not definitive.

questions.keyPoint: Prostaglandin E1 side effects: FAVE — Fever, Apnoea (most serious → may need intubation), Vasodilation/hypotension, Erythema (flushing). Always have resuscitation equipment available when starting PGE1.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old boy who had missed some of his recommended vaccinations presented to A&E with a fever and a sore throat. On examination he was drooling, sitting still and upright with an open mouth, with a soft inspiratory stridor. He looked scared.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Asthma: recurrent episodes of reversible airflow obstruction. Night-time/early morning cough and wheeze is characteristic (circadian variation in airway tone). Recurrent chest infections may reflect unrecognised/poorly controlled asthma. PEFR variability >20% = diagnostic.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Inhaled foreign body: sudden onset in well child, playing with older sibling (small toy/food). Unilateral wheeze/decreased air entry. Right bronchus more commonly affected (more vertical). CXR: unilateral hyperinflation or radio-opaque foreign body. Treatment: rigid bronchoscopy.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Acute epiglottitis (Haemophilus influenzae type B): classically in unvaccinated children. 'Tripod position' (sitting, leaning forward, drooling), muffled/hot potato voice, soft inspiratory stridor, toxic appearance. DO NOT examine throat — risk of laryngospasm. Call anaesthetics immediately.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Pertussis (Bordetella pertussis): 3 phases — catarrhal (coryzal, 1–2w), paroxysmal (paroxysmal cough + inspiratory whoop + post-tussive vomiting, 2–8w), convalescent. Lymphocytosis on FBC. Per-nasal swab or PCR. Erythromycin/azithromycin treatment.

questions.keyPoint: Epiglottitis vs Croup distinguishing features: Epiglottitis = sudden onset, toxic, no barking cough, drooling, NO distress if sitting still. Croup = gradual onset, barking seal cough, stridor worse at night, steeple sign on AP CXR.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old boy has been on holiday visiting his family in Asia. After his arrival home he has a persistent productive cough and cervical lymphadenopathy.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Laryngomalacia: congenital floppiness of supraglottic structures. Inspiratory stridor present from birth, worse when crying/feeding/supine, better prone. WELL child, growing normally. Diagnosis confirmed by flexible laryngoscopy (bronchoscopy). Resolves by 18–24 months in most.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: TB investigation: Mantoux/IGRA (screening) BUT for definitive diagnosis/identification of drug resistance = sputum culture (AFB smear + culture on Löwenstein-Jensen medium). Cervical lymphadenopathy = scrofula (TB lymphadenitis). In children: gastric washings if sputum not obtainable.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Bronchiolitis (RSV most likely): NPA (nasopharyngeal aspirate) for rapid RSV antigen detection/PCR. This allows cohort nursing and guides infection control. Bilateral crackles and wheeze in an infant = bronchiolitis.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis: the classic triad of faltering growth + malabsorption/steatorrhoea + recurrent chest infections in a young child = CF until proven otherwise. Sweat test (chloride >60 mmol/L) is the gold standard diagnostic test.

questions.keyPoint: CF investigations in order: Heel prick (IRT screen) → Sweat test (Cl >60 mEq/L = diagnostic) → CFTR gene analysis (ΔF508 most common). CXR shows hyperinflation, peribronchial thickening, bronchiectasis in advanced disease.

questions.stem: A 6-week-old baby girl was observed to have asymmetrical skin folds on her thighs. Ortolani's test was positive and an ultrasound scan confirmed the diagnosis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: DDH (developmental dysplasia of the hip): Pavlik harness or abduction splint for infants <6 months (diagnosed early). Holds hip in flexion and abduction to allow acetabular development. 95% success rate if started before 3 months.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Perthes disease (Legg-Calvé-Perthes): avascular necrosis of the femoral head. X-ray: increased density/fragmentation of femoral head, lateral subluxation. Treatment: physiotherapy + containment (abduction) + analgesia. Surgery if severe. Most recover with conservative management.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (brittle bone disease): blue sclerae (thin sclerae reveal underlying choroid), fragile bones. Type I (most common): autosomal dominant, COL1A1/COL1A2 mutation. Treatment: physiotherapy + bisphosphonates (pamidronate) to increase bone density. MUST DIFFERENTIATE FROM NAI.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Transient synovitis (irritable hip): most common cause of acute hip pain in children 3–10 years. Post-viral (often follows URTI). Normal inflammatory markers, small effusion. Treatment: NSAIDs + rest. Resolves spontaneously in 1–2 weeks. Kocher criteria distinguish from septic arthritis.

questions.keyPoint: Hip pathology by age: Neonate = DDH; <6 years = Transient synovitis or Perthes; 10–14 years = Perthes or SUFE; Adolescent = SUFE (obese male) or stress fracture. Each age group has its 'most likely' — know them!

choices.label: C

Type I (most common): autosomal dominant, COL1A1/COL1A2 mutation. Treatment: physiotherapy + bisphosphonates (pamidronate) to increase bone density. MUST DIFFERENTIATE FROM NAI.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Transient synovitis (irritable hip): most common cause of acute hip pain in children 3–10 years. Post-viral (often follows URTI). Normal inflammatory markers, small effusion. Treatment: NSAIDs + rest. Resolves spontaneously in 1–2 weeks. Kocher criteria distinguish from septic arthritis.

questions.keyPoint: Hip pathology by age: Neonate = DDH; <6 years = Transient synovitis or Perthes; 10–14 years = Perthes or SUFE; Adolescent = SUFE (obese male) or stress fracture. Each age group has its 'most likely' — know them!

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with unilateral hip pain. His X-ray reveals increased density of the femoral head.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Perthes disease (Legg-Calvé-Perthes): avascular necrosis of the femoral head. X-ray: increased density/fragmentation of femoral head, lateral subluxation. Treatment: physiotherapy + containment (abduction) + analgesia. Surgery if severe. Most recover with conservative management.

questions.stem: An 11-year-old girl presents with multiple brown spots on her back and a marked scoliosis.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Congenital talipes equinovarus (CTEV/club foot): cannot be passively corrected (rigid). Involves equinus + varus + adduction + supination. Treatment: Ponseti method (serial casting from 1st week of life) has revolutionised management. 95% success without surgery.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Neurofibromatosis type 1: autosomal dominant, NF1 gene (17q). Café-au-lait spots (≥6, >5mm) + neurofibromas + Lisch nodules (iris hamartomas) + scoliosis/kyphosis. Optic glioma most common CNS tumour. NF2 = bilateral vestibular schwannomas.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Positional talipes: due to intrauterine moulding. Foot appears inverted but can be passively corrected to neutral (unlike true CTEV which cannot). Treatment: stretching exercises by parents. Resolves spontaneously.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency rickets: knock-knees (genu valgum) + lethargy + bowing of long bones + frontal bossing + Harrison's sulcus + hypocalcaemia (seizures). ALP raised, phosphate low, PTH high, Vit D low. Treatment: vitamin D + calcium supplementation.

questions.keyPoint: Talipes memory: CAVE = Cavus/CTEV is Cannot correct (rigid). Calcaneovalgus = Correctable (flexible, postural). CTEV = fixed deformity needing Ponseti casting within days of birth.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old girl presents with joint pain, headache, muscle soreness, fever and malaise. She also has a circular, outwardly expanding rash with target lesions.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Septic arthritis in this age group: Staphylococcus aureus most common >6 months. Under 3 months: Group B Strep or E. coli. Neonates: S. aureus or gram-negatives. Orthopaedic emergency: joint washout + IV antibiotics to prevent AVN.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Reactive arthritis post-Campylobacter: joint pain following gastroenteritis is reactive arthritis. Campylobacter is a known trigger. Also associated with: Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia (all enteric pathogens). HLA-B27 positive patients more susceptible.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Rheumatic fever (Group A Streptococcus): Jones criteria — Major: carditis (ECG abnormality), polyarthritis, chorea, erythema marginatum, subcutaneous nodules. Evidence of preceding GAS infection. ASO titre elevated. Prophylactic penicillin prevents recurrence.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Lyme disease (Borrelia afzelii/burgdorferi): Erythema migrans = pathognomonic — expanding annular/target rash at tick bite site. Joint pain (Lyme arthritis), headache, myalgia, fatigue. Europe = B. afzelii. Treatment: amoxicillin/doxycycline.

questions.keyPoint: Reactive arthritis triggers memory: 'Can't see (conjunctivitis) + Can't pee (urethritis) + Can't climb a tree (arthritis)' = Classic Reiter's post-Chlamydia. For enteric triggers: Campylobacter, Salmonella, Shigella, Yersinia — the 4 Ss + C.

choices.label: A

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Reactive arthritis (Reiter's syndrome) post-Chlamydia: Can't see, Can't pee, Can't bend the knee. Chlamydia trachomatis is the classic trigger for sexually transmitted reactive arthritis. OCP doesn't protect against STIs. NAAT testing.

questions.stem: A 13-year-old male footballer presents with knee pain.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease: traction apophysitis at tibial tuberosity insertion of patellar tendon. Active adolescent male, unilateral or bilateral, pain/swelling at tibial tubercle. Exacerbated by sport. Self-limiting but may take months-years. Treatment: activity modification, quadriceps stretching, NSAIDs.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Kawasaki disease: fever >5 days + ≥4 of: bilateral non-purulent conjunctivitis, mucosal changes (strawberry tongue, red lips), rash, cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5cm), extremity changes (swollen feet → desquamation). Coronary aneurysms (and MI) = most feared complication.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Still's disease (Systemic JIA): youngest children, polyarticular, high spiking fevers, salmon rash, organomegaly, lymphadenopathy, high ferritin. Distinguished from Kawasaki by age pattern and joint involvement pattern. Macrophage activation syndrome (MAS) is a life-threatening complication.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Juvenile psoriatic arthritis: psoriatic features (scaly rash, nail pitting) + dactylitis (sausage digit) + arthritis. Can precede skin disease in children. RF negative. Treatment: NSAIDs, methotrexate, biologics (TNF inhibitors).

questions.keyPoint: Kawasaki diagnosis: Fever ≥5 days PLUS at least 4 of the 5 criteria (CRASH): Conjunctivitis bilateral, Rash polymorphous, Adenopathy cervical unilateral, Strawberry tongue/red lips/mucositis, Hand/foot changes (oedema → desquamation). Echo at diagnosis, 2 and 6 weeks for coronary aneurysms.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl presents with abdominal pain, pallor and an abdominal mass. There are raised levels of catecholamines in her urine.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Wilms' tumour (nephroblastoma): associated with WAGR syndrome: Wilms' + Aniridia + Genitourinary anomalies + Retardation (intellectual disability). Also Beckwith-Wiedemann and Denys-Drash syndrome. Large abdominal mass in a child = Wilms' until proven otherwise. WT1 gene.

choices.label: F

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl presents to the hospital with a history of viral infection a few weeks ago, and has now developed petechiae, purpura and epistaxis. Blood tests reveal severe thrombocytopenia. She is otherwise well and is not pale.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Aplastic anaemia: pancytopenia (anaemia + thrombocytopenia + neutropenia). Subdural from thrombocytopenia-induced bleeding. Not jaundiced (not haemolytic). Causes: idiopathic, Fanconi anaemia, drugs, viral. Treatment: HSCT (young patients) or immunosuppression (ATG + ciclosporin).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Iron deficiency anaemia: most common nutritional deficiency worldwide. Pica (eating non-food items like dirt/ice) is a feature of iron deficiency. Developmental delay can occur. Microcytic hypochromic anaemia, low ferritin, high TIBC. Treat with iron supplementation.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: ITP (immune thrombocytopenic purpura): post-viral (2–6 weeks after URTI/vaccination), isolated thrombocytopenia, NO anaemia, NO organomegaly. Autoantibodies (anti-GPIIb/IIIa) destroy platelets. Usually self-limiting. Treat if platelets <20 or bleeding: IVIG or steroids.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: EBV (infectious mononucleosis): fever + pharyngitis + lymphadenopathy + atypical lymphocytes (activated CD8 T cells) + splenomegaly/hepatomegaly. Monospot (heterophile antibody) positive. Avoid amoxicillin (rash in 90%). Avoid contact sports (splenic rupture risk).

questions.keyPoint: ITP vs TTP distinction: ITP = isolated thrombocytopenia, no anaemia, post-viral, children. TTP = thrombocytopenia + microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + neurological symptoms + renal failure + fever (pentad). TTP is life-threatening — treat with plasma exchange.

questions.stem: A 12-year-old girl presents with fever, sore throat, enlarged lymph nodes, mild hepatomegaly and lethargy. Blood tests reveal an increased number of white blood cells and atypical lymphocytes.

choices.label: B

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Sickle cell anaemia: at ~6 months, HbF replaced by HbS. First crises emerge. Haemolytic jaundice + microcytic hypochromic appearance (target cells). Angola = high sickle cell prevalence. However, note sickle cell is normocytic-normochromic — if microcytic, may have co-existing iron deficiency or α-thalassaemia trait.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents to A&E shocked and unable to extend his right hip. He requires aggressive fluid resuscitation. He previously had to be admitted overnight after having a tooth removed.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Haemophilia A: absent/reduced Factor VIII. Dental extraction requiring overnight admission + spontaneous/severe bleeding into hip joint (haemarthrosis) = classic presentation. Prolonged APTT, normal PT and bleeding time. Treatment: recombinant Factor VIII.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Anaemia of chronic disease (normocytic normochromic): in chronic inflammatory conditions (JIA, IBD, SLE). Trapped iron in macrophages → low serum iron, low TIBC (distinguishes from IDA), normal/high ferritin. Treat underlying condition.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Beta-thalassaemia major (Cooley's anaemia): severe haemolytic anaemia requiring regular transfusions. Elevated HbA2 and HbF (compensatory). Splenomegaly, facial bossing, 'hair-on-end' skull X-ray (extramedullary haematopoiesis). Iron overload from transfusions → chelation therapy.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Polycythaemia (elevated Hb and haematocrit): secondary to chronic cyanotic congenital heart disease. Clubbing + cyanotic murmur + hypertension + headaches = compensatory polycythaemia (RBC production increases to maintain O2 delivery despite low saturations). Symptoms from hyperviscosity.

questions.keyPoint: Anaemia of chronic disease vs iron deficiency: ACD = low iron, LOW TIBC, NORMAL/HIGH ferritin. IDA = low iron, HIGH TIBC, LOW ferritin. Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — it can be falsely normal/high in ACD masking co-existing IDA.

choices.label: A

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with a large haematoma in his thigh after a fall, which despite all measures cannot be controlled. He is known to have Christmas disease.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile neutropenia unresponsive to antibiotics after 7 days → add antifungal cover. Amphotericin B (or caspofungin/voriconazole) for empirical antifungal therapy. Candida and Aspergillus are the main fungal pathogens in neutropenic patients.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand disease (vWD): most common inherited bleeding disorder. Prolonged bleeding time (platelet plug formation impaired) + normal PT/APTT (coagulation cascade intact). Treatment for mild-moderate vWD type 1: desmopressin (DDAVP) releases stored vWF from endothelium.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Sickle cell dactylitis (hand-foot syndrome): painful vaso-occlusive crisis involving the small bones of hands/feet, typically first presentation in infants/toddlers with SCD. Treatment: IV fluids, analgesia (paracetamol → NSAIDs → opioids escalating), warmth, oxygen.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Christmas disease = Haemophilia B = Factor IX deficiency (not Factor VIII). Recombinant Factor IX is the specific treatment. Haemophilia A = Factor VIII deficiency. 'A = Factor VIII (8, eight letters). B = Factor IX (9, nine letters) = Christmas'.

questions.keyPoint: Haemophilia treatment memory: Haemophilia A (VIII deficiency) → Factor VIII replacement OR desmopressin (mild A). Haemophilia B (IX deficiency) = Christmas disease → Factor IX. VWD → Desmopressin (type 1) or VWF concentrate (type 2/3).

questions.stem: A 4-year-old girl who is having chemotherapy for osteosarcoma presents with a fever of 39°C and neutropenia. She has been treated with two different combinations of broad-spectrum antibiotics for a total of 7 days, but still has a raised temperature.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile neutropenia unresponsive to antibiotics after 7 days → add antifungal cover. Amphotericin B (or caspofungin/voriconazole) for empirical antifungal therapy. Candida and Aspergillus are the main fungal pathogens in neutropenic patients.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand disease (vWD): most common inherited bleeding disorder. Prolonged bleeding time (platelet plug formation impaired) + normal PT/APTT (coagulation cascade intact). Treatment for mild-moderate vWD type 1: desmopressin (DDAVP) releases stored vWF from endothelium.

choices.label: I

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia): most common childhood malignancy. Pancytopenia (bone marrow replacement by blasts) + lymphocytosis with blast cells. Treatment: BFM/UKALL protocols — induction (vincristine + steroids + asparaginase) → consolidation → maintenance (2–3 years).

questions.keyPoint: Haemophilia treatment memory: Haemophilia A (VIII deficiency) → Factor VIII replacement OR desmopressin (mild A). Haemophilia B (IX deficiency) = Christmas disease → Factor IX. VWD → Desmopressin (type 1) or VWF concentrate (type 2/3).

questions.stem: A 13-year-old girl suffers from severe menorrhagia and is told that she has a blood disorder which is causing her heavy periods. Investigations reveal a normal INR and APTT but a prolonged bleeding time.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Febrile neutropenia unresponsive to antibiotics after 7 days → add antifungal cover. Amphotericin B (or caspofungin/voriconazole) for empirical antifungal therapy. Candida and Aspergillus are the main fungal pathogens in neutropenic patients.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand disease (vWD): most common inherited bleeding disorder. Prolonged bleeding time (platelet plug formation impaired) + normal PT/APTT (coagulation cascade intact). Treatment for mild-moderate vWD type 1: desmopressin (DDAVP) releases stored vWF from endothelium.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Sickle cell dactylitis (hand-foot syndrome): painful vaso-occlusive crisis involving the small bones of hands/feet, typically first presentation in infants/toddlers with SCD. Treatment: IV fluids, analgesia (paracetamol → NSAIDs → opioids escalating), warmth, oxygen.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: ALL (Acute Lymphoblastic Leukaemia): most common childhood malignancy. Pancytopenia (bone marrow replacement by blasts) + lymphocytosis with blast cells. Treatment: BFM/UKALL protocols — induction (vincristine + steroids + asparaginase) → consolidation → maintenance (2–3 years).

questions.stem: A newborn baby has ambiguous genitalia identified shortly after birth.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Moderate unilateral antenatal hydronephrosis: outpatient US at 4–6 weeks post-birth (not immediately, as early neonatal dehydration/oliguria can give false negative). Prophylactic antibiotics meanwhile. If confirmed, MCUG/MAG3 depending on severity.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: UTI in infancy with sepsis: after acute treatment and normal US, MCUG is required (routine, not urgent) to look for vesicoureteric reflux. NICE guidance: febrile UTI <6 months → US during acute illness + MCUG at 4–6 weeks. Normal US doesn't exclude VUR.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Ambiguous genitalia: urgent chromosomal analysis to determine genetic sex. Also measure 17-OH progesterone (congenital adrenal hyperplasia), androgens, karyotype. A multidisciplinary team approach is essential. Avoid gender assignment until investigations complete.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Unilateral undescended testis in a newborn: no immediate investigation needed. If unilateral, most descend by 3 months. Refer to surgery at 6 months if still undescended. Bilateral undescended testes → urgent chromosomal analysis (may be female with CAH). Orchidopexy at 6–18 months.

questions.keyPoint: UTI investigation in children (NICE 2007/updated): <3 months: US during acute + consider MCUG. 3 months–3 years: US if responds to treatment. Atypical or recurrent UTI at any age: US + DMSA + MCUG. DMSA identifies renal scarring.

questions.stem: A newborn baby girl had antenatally diagnosed moderate unilateral renal pelvis dilatation.

choices.label: B

choices.label: D

questions.stem: An 8-year-old girl presents with fever, dysuria and difficulty in passing urine. Urine multistick showed 3+ leukocytes and was positive for nitrites.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome: heavy proteinuria (>3.5g/day, 3+ on dipstick) + hypoalbuminaemia + oedema. Periorbital oedema first (loose tissue), then generalised oedema/ascites. Serum albumin is the key investigation confirming hypoalbuminaemia. Most common cause in children: minimal change disease (steroid-responsive).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Diabetes insipidus: polyuria + pale/dilute urine with NORMAL dipstick (no glucose, no protein). Paired osmolality: serum osmolality high, urine osmolality inappropriately low (<300 mOsm/kg). Water deprivation test confirms DI. dDAVP distinguishes central from nephrogenic DI.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (nephritic syndrome): 1–3 weeks after GAS throat/skin infection → haematuria (dark 'Coca-Cola' urine) + hypertension + oedema + proteinuria. Elevated ASO titre confirms preceding streptococcal infection. Low C3 complement.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: UTI: positive nitrites + leukocytes = highly likely UTI. Definitive diagnosis requires urine microscopy and culture (MSU clean catch or SPA). Nitrites are produced by gram-negative bacteria (E. coli). Culture confirms organism and guides antibiotic choice.

choices.label: I
choices.text: Full blood count and blood film

questions.keyPoint: Nephrotic vs Nephritic syndrome: Nephrotic = massive Proteinuria + hypAlbuminaemia + Oedema + hyperlipidaemia (PALO). Nephritic = haematuria + hypertension + oliguria + mild proteinuria (HHO). Nephrotic is 'leaky' (protein leaks out). Nephritic is 'inflammatory' (glomerulonephritis).

questions.stem: A 12-month-old boy presents with faltering growth and swollen wrists. Wrist X-rays show cupping and fraying of the metaphyseal region. Blood tests: ALP raised, phosphate low, calcium normal, PTH normal, 25-hydroxy vitamin D normal.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Distal renal tubular acidosis (type I RTA): metabolic acidosis + hypokalaemia + urine pH >5.5 (CANNOT acidify urine). Nephrocalcinosis/nephrolithiasis (loin pain). Distal tubule fails to secrete H+. Associated with autoimmune diseases, Sjögren's syndrome. Treatment: sodium bicarbonate + potassium supplementation.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency rickets: low Vit D → low calcium → raised PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism) → phosphate wasting. Classic biochemical pattern. X-ray: widened growth plates, cupping/fraying of metaphyses. Treatment: vitamin D + calcium.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Primary hypoparathyroidism: low PTH → high phosphate, low calcium, normal Vit D, normal ALP. Dysmorphic features + hypocalcaemia in a neonate → consider DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion) — absent parathyroids + absent thymus + cardiac defects.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: X-linked hypophosphataemic rickets (XLH): PHEX gene mutation → elevated FGF23 → phosphaturia → low phosphate. Unlike nutritional rickets: Vitamin D NORMAL, PTH NORMAL, but phosphate persistently low. Treatment: phosphate supplements + calcitriol (not plain Vit D).

questions.keyPoint: RTA memory: Type I (Distal) = can't Drop the pH of urine (>5.5) + nephrocalcinosis/stones. Type II (Proximal) = Proximal wasting of bicarb, urine pH can fall <5.5. Type IV = hyperkalemia (aldosterone deficiency/resistance, associated with DM).

choices.label: C

questions.stem: A 3-year-old boy presents with a painful left testicle.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Phimosis with urinary retention: physiological phimosis is normal until ~4 years. Pathological phimosis causing urinary obstruction (ballooning, retention — palpable bladder) requires circumcision. First-line: topical steroids (betamethasone) but if obstructing → circumcision.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypospadias: ventral urethral opening. Advise AGAINST circumcision — the prepucial skin is used for surgical repair (urethroplasty). Perform urethroplasty at 6–18 months. Associated with undescended testis and inguinal hernia.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Advise the use of incontinence pads

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Testicular torsion: must be excluded in any male with acute scrotal/testicular pain — surgical emergency. No time for Doppler US in equivocal cases. Exploratory surgery (orchidopexy if viable, orchidectomy if infarcted) within 6 hours for best outcomes. 'Time is testicle'.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Hydrocele: transilluminates, testicle felt separately, non-tender. Communicating hydrocele has a patent processus vaginalis — most resolve spontaneously by 2 years. No urgent intervention. If persists beyond 2 years or increases in size → surgical repair (not 'L = None of the above' in this context, but this answer is 'None of the above' = no acute intervention).

questions.stem: A newborn boy has a swelling of the left scrotal sac. The left testicle can be felt within the swelling. The swelling transilluminates.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Phimosis with urinary retention: physiological phimosis is normal until ~4 years. Pathological phimosis causing urinary obstruction (ballooning, retention — palpable bladder) requires circumcision. First-line: topical steroids (betamethasone) but if obstructing → circumcision.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Hypospadias: ventral urethral opening. Advise AGAINST circumcision — the prepucial skin is used for surgical repair (urethroplasty). Perform urethroplasty at 6–18 months. Associated with undescended testis and inguinal hernia.

questions.stem: An 11-year-old boy presents with extensive burns caused by a house fire. He is alert but is crying and has a hoarse voice. Some of the burns are full thickness.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Meningococcal disease: non-blanching purpuric rash + fever = meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise. IV ceftriaxone immediately (or IM benzylpenicillin if in community pre-hospital). Do not delay antibiotics for LP.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Inhalation injury with hoarse voice: early intubation before airway oedema makes it impossible. Hoarseness indicates supraglottic involvement. This is the critical time to secure the airway. Do not wait. Delay = loss of airway.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Intraosseous needle in the distal tibia

choices.label: I
choices.text: Intraosseous needle in the proximal tibia
choices.explanation: Intraosseous access: when IV access fails in an emergency in a child, IO access is the next step. Proximal tibia (anteromedial surface, 1–2 cm below tibial tuberosity) is the preferred site in infants/children. EZ-IO or standard IO needle. ALL drugs and fluids can be given IO.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Major trauma with haemodynamic instability: FAST (Focussed Assessment with Sonography in Trauma) scan is the immediate bedside investigation for haemoperitoneum/haemothorax/pericardial tamponade. This guides whether emergency surgery/intervention is needed.

questions.keyPoint: Choking algorithm: Effective cough → encourage coughing, observe. Ineffective cough + conscious → 5 back blows between shoulder blades, alternate with 5 chest thrusts (infant) or 5 abdominal thrusts (child >1 year). Unconscious → CPR.

choices.label: A

questions.keyPoint: Choking algorithm: Effective cough → encourage coughing, observe. Ineffective cough + conscious → 5 back blows between shoulder blades, alternate with 5 chest thrusts (infant) or 5 abdominal thrusts (child >1 year). Unconscious → CPR.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old girl suddenly develops stridor and a barking cough 90 minutes after eating her dinner. She is now struggling to breathe, with marked recession. Her oxygen saturations are 85% in air.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Periorbital/orbital cellulitis: preseptal or postseptal. IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or ceftriaxone) for orbital cellulitis (proptosis + reduced eye movement). This is NOT allergic — it's bacterial infection of the periorbital tissues. IV antibiotics are the correct management.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mild-moderate allergic reaction (no systemic involvement, well child): antihistamine (chlorphenamine) is first-line. Observe. If deteriorates → IM adrenaline. Anaphylaxis requires IM adrenaline + IV fluids + antihistamine + steroids. Mild allergy without systemic features does not require adrenaline.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Anaphylaxis: IM adrenaline (epinephrine) is first-line treatment. Route = IM mid-outer thigh (lateral aspect). Adrenaline auto-injector (EpiPen) in community. IV adrenaline only in cardiac arrest setting. Also: high-flow oxygen, IV fluids, chlorphenamine, hydrocortisone (IV).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Anaphylaxis with stridor/laryngeal oedema OR severe croup: nebulised adrenaline (epinephrine) is indicated for both. This is a post-prandial onset of severe stridor — most likely anaphylaxis. Nebulised adrenaline 0.5 ml/kg of 1:1000 (max 5 ml) + IM adrenaline for anaphylaxis.

questions.keyPoint: Anaphylaxis management: Adrenaline IM (0.01 mg/kg of 1:1000) → FIRST AND MOST IMPORTANT. Then: O2 + IV access + fluid bolus + chlorphenamine IV + hydrocortisone IV. Steroids prevent biphasic reaction (delayed second episode 4–12h later).

choices.label: A

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Hyperventilation syndrome: respiratory alkalosis (high pH, low CO2, normal HCO3). Normal ammonia rules out metabolic cause. Breathing into a paper bag rebreathes CO2 to correct the alkalosis. Anxiety/panic attacks in school setting is the typical trigger.

questions.stem: A 13-month-old girl presents with a tonic-clonic seizure lasting for 10 minutes. Her parents inform you that she has had diarrhoea and vomiting for 24 hours and is refusing meals. Her blood glucose level was found to be 0.5 mmol/l.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DKA: metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3, negative base excess) + compensatory respiratory alkalosis (low CO2 = Kussmaul breathing). Dehydration + breathlessness in a diabetic. Management: careful IV fluids + insulin infusion. Avoid rapid fluid boluses.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fat oxidation disorder (MCAD deficiency most common): fasting + illness → inability to use fat as energy → severe hypoglycaemia. Presents after prolonged fasting or illness. Normal between episodes. Neonatal screening now includes MCAD. Treatment: avoid fasting, IV dextrose during illness.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Cerebral oedema complicating DKA treatment: most dangerous complication of DKA management. Risk factors: excess/rapid fluid administration, young age, new-onset DKA, severe dehydration. Presents with worsening neurological status 4–12h into treatment. Treat: 0.5–1 g/kg mannitol IV or hypertonic saline.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Addisonian crisis: hypotension + dehydration + hyponatraemia + metabolic acidosis. Hypoadrenalism → lack of cortisol and aldosterone → sodium wasting, potassium retention, hypoglycaemia. Emergency: IV hydrocortisone + IV saline + glucose. Trigger: infection, surgery, stress.

questions.keyPoint: DKA cerebral oedema red flags: sudden headache + confusion + falling GCS during treatment. Risk is HIGHER with rapid fluid replacement, bicarbonate administration, and young age. NEVER give bicarbonate in DKA (worsens cerebral oedema risk and causes paradoxical CSF acidosis).

questions.stem: A 3-day-old boy presents very unwell with a capillary refill time of 6 seconds. He appears very quiet and is dehydrated. He was born by a normal delivery after prolonged rupture of membranes, and his newborn check was normal apart from the fact that he had hypospadias and bilaterally undescended testes.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DKA: metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3, negative base excess) + compensatory respiratory alkalosis (low CO2 = Kussmaul breathing). Dehydration + breathlessness in a diabetic. Management: careful IV fluids + insulin infusion. Avoid rapid fluid boluses.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Congenital adrenal hyperplasia (CAH): 21-hydroxylase deficiency most common. Salt-wasting crisis in males (ambiguous genitalia may be missed as males can appear normal at birth). Hypospadias + undescended testes = abnormal genital differentiation. Life-threatening salt-wasting crisis day 1–4.

questions.keyPoint: DKA cerebral oedema red flags: sudden headache + confusion + falling GCS during treatment. Risk is HIGHER with rapid fluid replacement, bicarbonate administration, and young age. NEVER give bicarbonate in DKA (worsens cerebral oedema risk and causes paradoxical CSF acidosis).

questions.keyPoint: DKA cerebral oedema red flags: sudden headache + confusion + falling GCS during treatment. Risk is HIGHER with rapid fluid replacement, bicarbonate administration, and young age. NEVER give bicarbonate in DKA (worsens cerebral oedema risk and causes paradoxical CSF acidosis).

questions.stem: A 14-year-old girl presents after a fainting episode. She has a BP of 80/50 mmHg, and appears to be 10–15% dehydrated. Her blood gas shows pH 7.25, CO2 4.0 kPa and HCO3 18 mmol/l. Her sodium level is 125 mmol/l.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: DKA: metabolic acidosis (low pH, low HCO3, negative base excess) + compensatory respiratory alkalosis (low CO2 = Kussmaul breathing). Dehydration + breathlessness in a diabetic. Management: careful IV fluids + insulin infusion. Avoid rapid fluid boluses.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Fat oxidation disorder (MCAD deficiency most common): fasting + illness → inability to use fat as energy → severe hypoglycaemia. Presents after prolonged fasting or illness. Normal between episodes. Neonatal screening now includes MCAD. Treatment: avoid fasting, IV dextrose during illness.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Cerebral oedema complicating DKA treatment: most dangerous complication of DKA management. Risk factors: excess/rapid fluid administration, young age, new-onset DKA, severe dehydration. Presents with worsening neurological status 4–12h into treatment. Treat: 0.5–1 g/kg mannitol IV or hypertonic saline.
