questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with a spiral humeral fracture. Her mother says that she tripped over the back step, but the girl looks nervous and says 'No I didn't, it was on purpose.'

choices.label: A
choices.text: Ask the child if mummy hurt them

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Normal accidental bruising: bruises over bony prominences (shins, knees, forehead in a toddler) in ambulatory children are EXPECTED. Different colours = different ages (consistent with story). History is plausible. Discharge with follow-up and clinical vigilance. Bruising ONLY in pre-mobile infants (<6 months) should raise concerns.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Phone the Children's Social Care Team
choices.explanation: Non-accidental injury (NAI): a child's clear statement that the injury was intentional, combined with inconsistent history and a concerning fracture pattern → immediate referral to Children's Social Care. Spiral fractures in ambulatory children can be either accidental or non-accidental — the history is key.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Perform a blood test for full blood count
choices.explanation: Unexplained bruising with petechiae: full blood count to exclude haematological cause (thrombocytopenia, leukaemia, clotting disorder) before concluding NAI. Bruising in unusual places (not over bony prominences) is suspicious, but haematological causes must be excluded first. Petechiae + bruising = FBC urgent.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Abusive head trauma (shaken baby): unexplained seizure/apnoea/LOC in infant + bruising in unusual pattern (fingertip bruising from gripping). CT head is urgent — looks for subdural haematoma, DAI, cerebral oedema. Also: ophthalmology (retinal haemorrhages), skeletal survey.

choices.label: L
choices.text: Tell the parents that their child will probably be taken into care

questions.keyPoint: NAI fracture patterns high-specificity: Posterior rib fractures (gripping during shaking), Metaphyseal/corner fractures (shearing forces), Multiple fractures in different stages of healing. Any fracture in a non-mobile infant without clear accidental mechanism must trigger NAI investigation.

questions.stem: This is the leading cause of post-neonatal deaths in England and Wales.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Childhood cancer is the leading cause of death in children aged 5–10 years in England and Wales. Leukaemia (especially ALL) and CNS tumours are the most common childhood cancers. Injury is the second most common cause.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Pneumonia is the leading infectious cause of death in children under 5 years globally (WHO data). Responsible for ~15% of all deaths in this age group. Streptococcus pneumoniae and RSV are major causes.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Congenital abnormalities are the leading cause of post-neonatal (28 days–1 year) deaths in England and Wales, accounting for ~30% of infant deaths in this period. Includes structural heart defects, chromosomal abnormalities, neural tube defects.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Diarrhoeal disease is the second leading cause of death in children under 5 worldwide (after pneumonia/respiratory infections in some years). Rotavirus and E. coli are major causes. Oral rehydration therapy has dramatically reduced mortality.

questions.keyPoint: Global under-5 mortality causes (WHO): Preterm complications > Pneumonia > Intrapartum complications > Diarrhoea > Malaria. In UK: Congenital abnormalities (post-neonatal), then SUDI, then cancer (>1 year).

questions.stem: At her booking visit a 42-year-old pregnant mother asks you about antenatal screening. You discuss with her a particular condition for which a prenatal screening test is routinely offered nationally.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Down's syndrome antenatal screening: combined test at 11–13+6 weeks (nuchal translucency + PAPP-A + free β-hCG) gives risk ratio. Advanced maternal age (>35, especially >40) increases Down's risk. Diagnostic: chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Phenylketonuria (PKU): detected on Guthrie/blood spot test (elevated phenylalanine). Strict phenylalanine-restricted diet prevents intellectual disability. Diet must be maintained lifelong (or at least through childhood/pregnancy). Sapropterin (BH4) for some variants.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis (CF): IRT (immunoreactive trypsinogen) is elevated in CF neonates due to pancreatic duct obstruction. IRT positive → CFTR gene testing → if still equivocal → sweat test at 4–6 weeks. Autosomal recessive, CFTR gene, ΔF508 most common mutation.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy: NOT part of UK antenatal or neonatal screening programme. X-linked recessive. Carrier testing available for at-risk families. DMD diagnosis is typically at 2–5 years when motor delay becomes apparent.

questions.keyPoint: UK neonatal blood spot (heel prick) screening: 9 conditions — PKU, CH (congenital hypothyroidism), CF, SCD, MCADD, MSUD, IVA, GA1, HCU. Plus AABR hearing test. Plus clinical examination including red reflex, hip (Barlow/Ortolani), heart.

questions.stem: A pregnant mother has recently met a severely disabled teenager with this genetic condition, and is very concerned that her unborn child might have it. She wants to know whether it can be routinely screened for. You explain that it is not part of the national screening programme.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Down's syndrome antenatal screening: combined test at 11–13+6 weeks (nuchal translucency + PAPP-A + free β-hCG) gives risk ratio. Advanced maternal age (>35, especially >40) increases Down's risk. Diagnostic: chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis.

choices.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy: NOT part of UK antenatal or neonatal screening programme. X-linked recessive. Carrier testing available for at-risk families. DMD diagnosis is typically at 2–5 years when motor delay becomes apparent.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Congenital cataract: screened by red reflex examination using an ophthalmoscope (specialised equipment) as part of the routine neonatal physical examination. Absent/white red reflex = refer urgently. Must treat within weeks to prevent amblyopia.

questions.stem: Five children in the same nursery class contract the same disease. The GP says that it is not a notifiable condition.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mumps: orchitis occurs in post-pubertal males (20–30%), rarely causing infertility (bilateral orchitis required, occurs in <5% of orchitis cases). Mumps is a notifiable disease. MMR vaccine highly effective. Parotitis is the classic presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Chickenpox (varicella): NOT a notifiable disease in England (although it is in some other countries). Measles, mumps, rubella, meningitis, TB, whooping cough ARE notifiable. Chickenpox outbreaks in schools are common but not notifiable.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Measles: notifiable disease. Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa opposite molars) are pathognomonic, appearing 1–2 days before the rash. Rash spreads cephalocaudally. Complications: pneumonia, encephalitis, SSPE. Notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Malaria: notifiable disease. Travel to endemic area + fever + rigors + myalgia + headache = malaria until proven otherwise. Thick and thin blood films (×3). P. vivax most common in Indian subcontinent. Chloroquine ± primaquine for vivax.

questions.keyPoint: UK notifiable diseases memory aid: They need to be NOTIFIABLE to the PROPER OFFICER (local authority). Key ones: Acute encephalitis, Anthrax, Botulism, Brucellosis, Cholera, COVID-19, Diphtheria, EHEC, Food poisoning, HUS, Leprosy, Malaria, Measles, Meningitis (bacterial), Meningococcal disease, Mumps, Plague, Rabies, Rubella, Smallpox, TB, Typhus, VHF, WNV, Whooping cough, Yellow fever.

questions.stem: A 17-year-old boy contracts this illness. He sees his GP as he has read on the Internet that it could render him infertile, and he is very concerned about this.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mumps: orchitis occurs in post-pubertal males (20–30%), rarely causing infertility (bilateral orchitis required, occurs in <5% of orchitis cases). Mumps is a notifiable disease. MMR vaccine highly effective. Parotitis is the classic presentation.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Chickenpox (varicella): NOT a notifiable disease in England (although it is in some other countries). Measles, mumps, rubella, meningitis, TB, whooping cough ARE notifiable. Chickenpox outbreaks in schools are common but not notifiable.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Measles: notifiable disease. Koplik spots (white spots on buccal mucosa opposite molars) are pathognomonic, appearing 1–2 days before the rash. Rash spreads cephalocaudally. Complications: pneumonia, encephalitis, SSPE. Notifiable immediately on clinical suspicion.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Congenital rubella syndrome: maternal rubella in first trimester → congenital defects (IUFD or: cataract + sensorineural deafness + cardiac defects [PDA, pulmonary artery stenosis] + growth restriction + intellectual disability). Notifiable. MMR vaccination prevents.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents to A&E feeling generally unwell. He is complaining of a headache, myalgia and an intermittent fever over the last few days. He has recently returned from visiting relatives in India.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Mumps: orchitis occurs in post-pubertal males (20–30%), rarely causing infertility (bilateral orchitis required, occurs in <5% of orchitis cases). Mumps is a notifiable disease. MMR vaccine highly effective. Parotitis is the classic presentation.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Malaria: notifiable disease. Travel to endemic area + fever + rigors + myalgia + headache = malaria until proven otherwise. Thick and thin blood films (×3). P. vivax most common in Indian subcontinent. Chloroquine ± primaquine for vivax.

questions.stem: This term describes the number of children who develop Henoch-Schönlein purpura per year in the UK.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Incidence: number of NEW cases of a disease in a defined population over a defined time period. '…who DEVELOP HSP per year' = new cases over time = incidence. Prevalence = all existing cases at a point in time.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Specificity: ability of a test to correctly identify those WITHOUT the disease (true negative rate). 'Proportion of babies WITHOUT CF who have a NEGATIVE test' = TN/(TN+FP) = specificity. High specificity = few false positives = good confirmatory test.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Prevalence: number of existing (old + new) cases in a population at a specific POINT in time (or period). 'Children LIVING with CF in January 2010' = all existing cases at that time point = point prevalence.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Number needed to treat (NNT): NNT = 1/ARR. The number of patients you need to treat with an intervention to prevent one bad outcome. Lower NNT = more effective treatment. Example: NNT of 10 means treating 10 patients prevents 1 event.

questions.keyPoint: Statistics memory: Sensitivity = Sn-NOUT (Sensitive test, Negative result rules OUT disease). Specificity = Sp-PIN (Specific test, Positive result rules IN disease). PPV/NPV depend on PREVALENCE — a highly specific test has higher PPV in high-prevalence populations.

questions.topic: Causes of difficulty breathing — pneumonia, effusion, foreign body (2.8)
questions.optionsTitle: Causes of difficulty breathing — pneumonia, effusion, foreign body (2.8)
questions.stem: Rob, a 9-month-old boy, presents with fever and difficulty breathing for 3 days. His growth is normal. Examination shows widespread bilateral wheeze and crackles with subcostal recession.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bronchiolitis: the age (under 1 year), bilateral clinical signs and history are characteristic. RSV is the commonest cause. Heart failure is unlikely with no hepatomegaly. Signs are bilateral, excluding focal unilateral lesions.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Inhaled foreign body (left side): reduced air entry left side + apex displaced right = left bronchus obstruction with ball-valve hyper-expansion. 50% of children with inhaled FB have no history volunteered. Urgent rigid bronchoscopy under GA.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Right pleural effusion: persistent fever post-antibiotics for pneumonia + stony dull percussion + reduced breath sounds = parapneumonic effusion or empyema. Stony dullness differentiates effusion from simple pneumonia. Ultrasound confirms.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Right-sided pneumonia: classic signs — dullness to percussion, bronchial breathing, crepitations at the right base. Stony dullness (vs simple dullness) would suggest pleural fluid. Percussion and auscultation localise the pathology.

questions.keyPoint: Percussion guide: Resonant=normal. Dull=consolidation. STONY DULL=pleural effusion. Hyper-resonant=pneumothorax. Stony dullness is the key percussion finding that distinguishes pleural effusion from pneumonia.

questions.stem: Jamalah, a 7-year-old girl, has had 3 episodes of breathing difficulty with colds. Her mother notes she 'coughs and whistles' in the cold. Examination: RR 44/min, mild recession, hyper-resonant chest, bilateral wheeze.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Asthma: recurrent episodes with URTI triggers + interval symptoms (cold air, exercise) at age 7. Hyper-resonant chest = air trapping. Viral-induced wheeze (option M) applies to younger children (<5 years) with no interval symptoms. Asthma diagnosis: >3 episodes of wheeze + atopy + responsiveness to bronchodilators.

questions.keyPoint: Asthma vs viral-induced wheeze: Asthma=older child, interval symptoms between illnesses (exercise/cold air triggers), personal/family atopy, PEFR variability >20%. Viral-induced wheeze=<3 years, wheeze ONLY during URTI, NO interval symptoms, no atopy needed.

questions.stem: Rosette can build a three-cube tower and can point to her nose.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 6 weeks: social smile — one of the most constant early milestones. Absence by 8 weeks requires investigation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: 8 months: crawling typically begins around 8 months. 10-15% of normal children never crawl (bottom-shuffle instead) — not developmental delay.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: 12 months: first meaningful words. 'Dada' said specifically to father = meaningful first word (not babble).

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: 18 months: building a 3-block tower + pointing to body parts. Reflects fine motor and receptive language development.

questions.keyPoint: Milestones shortcut: 6W=social smile, 6M=sit with support, 8M=sit alone+transfers objects, 12M=walk+first word, 18M=3-block tower+points to body parts, 2Y=2-word phrases, 3Y=cooperative play+copies circle, 4Y=copies square, 5Y=ties shoelaces.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: 6 weeks: social smile — one of the most constant early milestones. Absence by 8 weeks requires investigation.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: 8 months: crawling typically begins around 8 months. 10-15% of normal children never crawl (bottom-shuffle instead) — not developmental delay.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: 12 months: first meaningful words. 'Dada' said specifically to father = meaningful first word (not babble).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: 5 years: tying shoelaces requires mature fine motor skills and procedural memory.

questions.keyPoint: Milestones shortcut: 6W=social smile, 6M=sit with support, 8M=sit alone+transfers objects, 12M=walk+first word, 18M=3-block tower+points to body parts, 2Y=2-word phrases, 3Y=cooperative play+copies circle, 4Y=copies square, 5Y=ties shoelaces.

questions.topic: Child health surveillance — age of first action in the UK (3.8)

questions.stem: First measles/mumps/rubella (MMR) immunization.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Newborn Hearing Screen (NBHS) performed ideally before hospital discharge. Early identification before 6 months allows intervention before critical language period.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Neonatal blood spot at 5-6 days. UK screens 9 conditions including PKU, congenital hypothyroidism, CF, sickle cell disease, and MCADD.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: MMR is given at 12 months in the UK (1st dose). Booster at 3 years 4 months (pre-school).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Vision screening by orthoptist at 4-5 years (pre-school) to detect amblyopia and strabismus. Critical period closes at approximately 7-8 years.

questions.keyPoint: UK newborn universal checks: (1) Newborn physical examination Day 1-3 (hips, heart, eyes/red reflex, genitalia, spine, palate). (2) Day 5-6: heel prick blood spot. (3) Newborn hearing screen (before discharge). These are the 3 pillars of UK newborn universal screening.

questions.stem: Clarissa, 20-month-old, is not walking. She sits at 10 months and crawls, dragging her right leg. She has always preferred her left hand. Examination: increased tone and brisk reflexes in the right arm and leg.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21): chromosome karyotype (or DNA FISH for rapid result in 1-2 days) confirms the diagnosis. FISH gives results for specific chromosomes rapidly; karyotype gives complete chromosomal information.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Congenital infection (TORCH syndrome): cataracts + hepatosplenomegaly + sensorineural hearing loss + microcephaly + maternal illness in pregnancy. CMV is the most common congenital infection in the developed world.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy: proximal muscle weakness causing difficulty with stairs. CK markedly elevated (often >20,000 IU/L). Calf pseudohypertrophy + Gower's sign. X-linked recessive. Confirm with dystrophin gene mutation analysis.

choices.label: F
choices.text: CT or MRI scan of the brain
choices.explanation: Spastic hemiplegia (cerebral palsy): early unilateral hand preference (<12 months) is a red flag. MRI brain identifies the underlying lesion (PVL, cortical infarction). MRI preferred over CT (no radiation, superior soft tissue detail).

choices.label: I
choices.text: Maternal amino acids for raised phenylalanine

questions.keyPoint: Developmental delay investigation framework: Chromosomes (karyotype/FISH for dysmorphic features), Thyroid (TFTs for hypothyroidism), Radiology (MRI brain for CP/structural), Infection (TORCH screen for congenital infection), Muscle enzymes (CK for DMD), EEG (seizures/regression).

questions.stem: Gerald, 4, born in Tanzania, had untreated severe neonatal jaundice. He has involuntary movements with unusual posturing; when startled he adopts an ATNR pattern. He is hypotonic when asleep.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Ataxic CP: cerebellar involvement causing broad-based gait, truncal ataxia, and intention tremor. Least common CP type (~5%). Exclude posterior fossa tumour, Friedreich's ataxia, and metabolic causes.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Choreoathetoid (dyskinetic) CP: caused by basal ganglia damage from kernicterus (bilirubin neurotoxicity to globus pallidus). Involuntary movements + persisting primitive reflexes (ATNR) + hypotonia during sleep with dyskinesia when awake.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Spastic diplegia: bilateral lower limb involvement from PVL (periventricular leukomalacia) in premature infants. Upper limbs mildly affected (can feed self). Scissoring gait + toe-walking. The corticospinal tracts to the legs run adjacent to the periventricles.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Right hemiplegia: increased tone/reflexes in right arm and leg = UMN lesion in the LEFT cerebral hemisphere. Early hand preference at 7 months (normal: 18-24 months) is a classic red flag for hemiplegia.

questions.keyPoint: CP motor types: 70-80% Spastic (UMN — high tone, brisk reflexes, Babinski+). 10-15% Dyskinetic (basal ganglia — involuntary movements, kernicterus cause). 5% Ataxic (cerebellar — wide-based gait, intention tremor). Always classify by topography (hemi/di/quad) AND GMFCS level (I-V).

questions.stem: Rachael, a 3-month-old, 5th child of a 39-year-old mother, has upslanting palpebral fissures, epicanthic folds, flat nasal bridge, single palmar crease, hypotonia, and heart failure signs (tachypnoea, sweating, hepatomegaly).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21): classic dysmorphic features. Advanced maternal age (39 years) increases risk. Congenital heart disease (AVSD, VSD) in 40-50% causes heart failure. Hepatomegaly in infants = right heart failure.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Fragile X syndrome: most common inherited intellectual disability in males. X-linked dominant (CGG repeat in FMR1 gene). Large head, prominent ears and jaw, macro-orchidism (post-pubertal). Maternal uncle affected = X-linked transmission pattern.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome (47XXY): tall stature, small firm testes, gynaecomastia, hypogonadism, infertility, learning and behavioural difficulties. Most diagnosed in adulthood (infertility). Testosterone replacement from puberty.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Patau syndrome (trisomy 13): cleft lip/palate + cutis aplasia (scalp defect) + polydactyly + holoprosencephaly/absent suck + congenital heart disease. Majority die within the first year.

questions.keyPoint: Trisomy memory: 21=Down's=LIFE (most common, longest survival). 18=Edwards'=Expires early (rocker-bottom feet, overlapping fingers, 90% die <1 year). 13=Patau=Perishes (midline defects, polydactyly, usually fatal). 13<18<21 in terms of survival.

questions.stem: Amelia, 14, has not started puberty. She is short (<0.4th centile). She had puffy hands and feet as a baby. She has a systolic murmur and diminished femoral pulses compared with brachial pulses.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Down syndrome (trisomy 21): classic dysmorphic features. Advanced maternal age (39 years) increases risk. Congenital heart disease (AVSD, VSD) in 40-50% causes heart failure. Hepatomegaly in infants = right heart failure.

choices.label: G

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Klinefelter syndrome (47XXY): tall stature, small firm testes, gynaecomastia, hypogonadism, infertility, learning and behavioural difficulties. Most diagnosed in adulthood (infertility). Testosterone replacement from puberty.

choices.label: M
choices.explanation: Turner syndrome (45X0): primary amenorrhoea, absent puberty, short stature, neonatal lymphoedema (puffy hands/feet). Coarctation of the aorta in 15-30% causes diminished femoral pulses and upper limb hypertension. Karyotype confirms.

questions.keyPoint: Trisomy memory: 21=Down's=LIFE (most common, longest survival). 18=Edwards'=Expires early (rocker-bottom feet, overlapping fingers, 90% die <1 year). 13=Patau=Perishes (midline defects, polydactyly, usually fatal). 13<18<21 in terms of survival.

questions.reference: EMQ — Care of the Sick Child & Accidents
questions.stem: Noel is a 7-day-old baby admitted with fever. He is feeding well from the breast. He has no obvious source for his fever.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Neonates with unexplained fever require IV antibiotics: reliable bioavailability, unpredictable oral absorption in neonates (gastric emptying rate, milk in stomach). Full septic screen first.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Oral liquid antibiotics for a well child with otitis media. No indication for IV once the child is well and tolerating oral intake. Oral amoxicillin suspension is the standard treatment.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Acute severe asthma: nebulized bronchodilators (salbutamol + ipratropium) deliver higher concentrations to the airways than MDI+spacer in acute severe attacks. Oxygen-driven nebulization preferred. IV agents for life-threatening asthma.

questions.keyPoint: IV antibiotics indicated ('SICK'): Septic/shocked, Immunocompromised, Cannot tolerate oral (vomiting), Known severe focus (meningitis, osteomyelitis, septic arthritis, empyema). Otherwise oral is as effective for most paediatric infections.

questions.reference: EMQ — Care of the Sick Child & Accidents
questions.stem: Luke is a 3-year-old boy in the paediatric assessment unit. He is eating and drinking well. He has a fever, tachypnoea, and focal chest signs. SpO2 96% in air.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Vincristine (a vesicant) must be given intravenously via central line (Hickman/portacath). Extravasation causes severe tissue necrosis. Central line ensures safe, reliable IV access.

choices.label: E

questions.reference: EMQ — Care of the Sick Child & Accidents
questions.stem: Nathaniel, 7-year-old, trauma victim. He is talking. HR 130/min, BP 80/40 mmHg, RR 30/min, SpO2 93% in air.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: She has an airway but it is compromised (blood, moaning/gasping). Airway opening manoeuvres (head tilt/chin lift or jaw thrust if C-spine injury suspected) BEFORE bag-mask ventilation. Clear secretions/blood first.

choices.label: F
choices.text: Cardiac compressions — both hands, one on top of the other
choices.explanation: Older child requiring CPR: two-handed compressions (one hand on top of the other), same as adult technique. Rate 100-120/min, depth 5 cm, allow full chest recoil.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Infant (<1 year) requiring CPR: encircling technique (two thumbs on lower third of sternum, both hands encircling chest) preferred. If single rescuer: two-finger technique.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Cardiac compressions — one hand on the sternum

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: He has a patent airway (talking). Low SpO2 → immediate high-flow oxygen (Breathing step of ABCDE). After O2, address Circulation (IV access + fluid for haemodynamic instability). Always: Airway → Breathing → Circulation in order.

questions.keyPoint: Paediatric CPR: Compression:ventilation = 15:2 (2-rescuer paediatric). Rate = 100-120/min. Depth = 4 cm infant, 5 cm child. Technique: Infant=encircling or 2-finger. Child=1-2 hands. Paediatric cardiac arrest is usually RESPIRATORY in origin — effective ventilation is critical.

questions.reference: EMQ — Care of the Sick Child & Accidents
questions.stem: Jacob, 15, found unconscious in a park after drinking large amounts of cider. Slurred breathing. Blood glucose 0.9 mmol/L.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Alcohol: causes hypoglycaemia (inhibits gluconeogenesis) + CNS/respiratory depression. Always check BM in an unconscious child. IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia + airway protection.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Button battery in oesophagus: 'halo sign' on X-ray is pathognomonic. SURGICAL EMERGENCY — liquefactive necrosis can begin within 2 hours via electrical current and alkali generation. Urgent endoscopic removal. Coins have a single ring (no halo).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Digoxin toxicity: bradycardia + hypotension + ECG changes (T wave abnormalities, AV block). Activated charcoal if <1 hour. Digibind (digoxin-specific Fab antibodies) for life-threatening toxicity.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: MDMA (Ecstasy): hyperthermia + SIADH-induced hyponatraemia (also excess water intake) + agitation + tachycardia + dilated pupils. Most dangerous features: hyperthermia and hyponatraemia. Active cooling + careful fluid management + benzodiazepines.

questions.keyPoint: Button battery emergency: oesophageal battery = <2 hours to severe injury. 'Halo/double ring' sign = button battery (vs coin = single ring). Management: straight to urgent endoscopy. No delay for MRI or further investigations.

questions.stem: Ilsa, 18 months, returns from her father's with two bite-shaped bruises: a small one on her hand (consistent with a child bite) and a larger elliptical mark on her upper arm. Father says she knocked herself on a coffee table.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: ITP: follows viral illness 2-6 weeks previously, widespread purpura/bruising, child is WELL (no fever, no hepatosplenomegaly, no pallor — distinguishes from leukaemia). FBC shows isolated thrombocytopenia. Self-limiting in most children.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Meningococcal septicaemia: non-blanching petechiae/purpura + fever + shock = EMERGENCY. Give IM/IV benzylpenicillin or IV ceftriaxone IMMEDIATELY. Do not delay antibiotics for LP or bloods. ABC resuscitation simultaneously.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Non-accidental injury (bite marks): larger upper arm bite with inter-canine distance >3 cm indicates an adult bite. Implausible explanation for bite marks = NAI. Forensic dentist may help confirm. Child protection referral mandatory.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome: exfoliative staphylococcal toxin (ETs A and B) causes intra-epidermal split. Nikolsky sign positive. IV flucloxacillin + supportive care. NOT non-accidental injury.

questions.keyPoint: NAI injury patterns: Bite marks (>3 cm inter-canine = adult), Cigarette burns (circular, punched-out, uniform), Immersion scalds (glove/stocking, sharp demarcation, no splash marks), Patterned bruises (match shape of implement), Posterior rib fractures (highly specific for gripping/squeezing in infants).

questions.stem: Danielle, 5, is well and afebrile with widespread bruising and petechiae over her legs and trunk. She had a viral URTI 2 weeks ago. Examination otherwise normal.

choices.label: D

choices.explanation: Non-accidental injury (forced immersion scald): 'glove/stocking' distribution of burn with sharp demarcation = feet held deliberately in hot water. Accidental scalds have irregular, non-demarcated margins with splash marks.

questions.stem: George, 2-day-old, born by forceps delivery. Feeding well, no risk factors for sepsis. He has a rash that keeps appearing in different places at different times.

choices.label: C

questions.stem: Mohammed, born at term, is 6 hours old with rapid breathing. He needed mask ventilation at birth. CXR: hypertranslucency on the left, left lung edge visible, mediastinum displaced to the right.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD): defined as oxygen dependency at >36 weeks postmenstrual age in a premature infant. Caused by barotrauma + oxygen toxicity. 'Spongy' CXR appearance. Wean oxygen gradually; diuretics; inhaled steroids; caffeine.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Congenital diaphragmatic hernia (CDH): bowel in the chest on CXR + respiratory distress from pulmonary hypoplasia + mediastinal shift. Most are left-sided. Avoid bag-mask ventilation (distends herniated bowel). Stabilise → surgical repair.

choices.label: G
choices.text: Persistent pulmonary hypertension of the newborn (PPHN)

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pneumothorax: spontaneous (1-2% of newborns) or related to positive pressure ventilation. CXR: hypertranslucency, visible lung edge, mediastinal shift AWAY from affected side. Treat with needle decompression or intercostal drain.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Tracheo-oesophageal fistula with oesophageal atresia: inability to pass NGT (coils in oesophageal pouch) + bilious vomiting + failure to pass meconium. CXR: NGT coil + gas in stomach (distal TOF). Urgent surgical repair. Associated with VACTERL syndrome.

questions.keyPoint: Neonatal respiratory distress timing: Immediate (0-2h)=CDH, pneumothorax, RDS (premature). First hours (2-12h)=TTN (post C-section, resolves 48h), PPHN (cyanosis with normal CXR). After 24h=pneumonia/sepsis, cardiac lesions. BPD=chronic, diagnosis at >36 weeks corrected GA.

questions.stem: Catherine, 12-year-old girl, is short (<1st centile) and has not started puberty. Her mid-parental height is on the 50th centile. BP is 135/65 mmHg.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Coeliac disease in Down syndrome: 5-10% prevalence. Weight falls more than height (malabsorption). Irritability + decreased appetite. Anti-tTG IgA + total IgA. Also screen for hypothyroidism.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Turner syndrome (45X0): short girl with delayed puberty → karyotype. Elevated BP may be from undetected coarctation of the aorta (associated with Turner syndrome). Karyotype is the first-line investigation.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Acquired hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's thyroiditis): centile crossing + lethargy + declining school performance + exercise intolerance. TSH is the screening test. Weight is often preserved or increased relative to height.

questions.keyPoint: Short stature investigation approach: First line — FBC, ESR, TFTs, coeliac screen, renal function. Second line — bone age X-ray (delayed bone age = constitutional delay, hypothyroidism, or GHD), karyotype in girls. Third line — IGF-1, GH provocation test, pituitary MRI.

questions.stem: Olu, 2, Nigerian origin, has bowed legs and widened wrists. He was exclusively breastfed for 6 months and has a limited diet. X-rays show metaphyseal cupping and fraying.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Cow's milk protein allergy (CMPA): faltering growth + GI symptoms + eczema after formula introduction. Non-IgE mediated CMPA most common in infants. Switch to extensively hydrolysed formula (or amino acid formula for severe). Resolves in 80% by age 3-5 years.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Kwashiorkor: severe protein malnutrition WITH oedema (hypoalbuminaemia). Oedema masks the weight deficit. Features: depigmented hair, 'flaky paint' dermatosis, hepatomegaly. Distinguish from marasmus (pure caloric deficiency: wasting, NO oedema).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Vitamin D deficiency rickets: dark skin + limited dietary intake. Bowed legs, widened wrists, rachitic rosary. X-ray: metaphyseal cupping and fraying (pathognomonic). Raised ALP, low phosphate. Treat with vitamin D + calcium.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Vitamin K deficiency (haemorrhagic disease of the newborn): prolonged PT (vitamin K-dependent factors II, VII, IX, X) with normal APTT. Breast milk has insufficient vitamin K. IV/IM vitamin K treats acutely. Prevention: prophylactic vitamin K at birth.

questions.keyPoint: Kwashiorkor vs Marasmus: Kwashiorkor = protein-deficient → OEDEMA + 'flaky paint' skin + fatty liver. Marasmus = total caloric deficiency → severe WASTING, 'skin and bone', NO oedema. Memory: Kwashiorkor='Kwelling (swelling)'. Marasmus='Must wasting'.

questions.stem: Harry, 5, has 3 days of cough, coryza and conjunctivitis then high fever + Koplik spots on buccal mucosa + maculopapular rash starting behind the ears, spreading cephalocaudally.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: EBV (infectious mononucleosis): amoxicillin/ampicillin rash in ~90% of EBV patients given aminopenicillins (immune-complex mediated). Features: exudative pharyngitis, cervical lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, atypical lymphocytes, Monospot positive.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Measles: the 3 C's prodrome (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis) + Koplik spots (pathognomonic white spots on buccal mucosa) + cephalocaudal rash with high fever on day 4. Notifiable disease, MMR-preventable.

choices.label: P
choices.explanation: Roseola infantum (HHV-6, 'sixth disease'): fever 3-5 days → fever RESOLVES → macular rash appears on trunk. Age 6-24 months. Rash appears AS FEVER BREAKS. Common cause of febrile seizures.

choices.label: S
choices.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus toxic shock syndrome: TSST-1 exotoxin from wound infection causes high fever, diffuse erythematous rash (strawberry tongue), hypotension, multi-organ dysfunction. IV flucloxacillin + IV fluid resuscitation.

questions.keyPoint: Exanthem timing: Roseola (HHV-6)=rash AFTER fever breaks. Measles=rash WITH fever day 4 (cephalocaudal). Scarlet fever (GAS)=rash DURING throat infection fever. Chickenpox=rash WITH low-grade fever (centripetal). Fifth disease (parvovirus B19)=rash after mild illness (slapped cheek).

choices.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus toxic shock syndrome: TSST-1 exotoxin from wound infection causes high fever, diffuse erythematous rash (strawberry tongue), hypotension, multi-organ dysfunction. IV flucloxacillin + IV fluid resuscitation.

questions.keyPoint: Exanthem timing: Roseola (HHV-6)=rash AFTER fever breaks. Measles=rash WITH fever day 4 (cephalocaudal). Scarlet fever (GAS)=rash DURING throat infection fever. Chickenpox=rash WITH low-grade fever (centripetal). Fifth disease (parvovirus B19)=rash after mild illness (slapped cheek).

questions.stem: Samuel, 9, has had recurrent severe chest infections since infancy and has poor weight gain. CXR: large-volume lungs with reticulonodular shadowing, peribronchial thickening, and hilar lymphadenopathy.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Cystic fibrosis: recurrent severe chest infections from infancy + malabsorption (poor weight gain). CXR in established CF: hyperinflation, peribronchial thickening, bronchiectasis, hilar lymphadenopathy. Sweat test confirms diagnosis (Cl >60 mmol/L).

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Inhaled foreign body: sudden onset during eating + focal unilateral wheeze + radio-opaque FB on CXR. Right main bronchus more commonly affected. Urgent rigid bronchoscopy under GA.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Obstructive sleep apnoea: Down syndrome is a major risk factor (midface hypoplasia, macroglossia, hypotonia). Loud snoring + EDS + large tonsils. Overnight sleep study (polysomnography) confirms. Treatment: tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Pneumonia: fever + tachypnoea + focal chest signs. Loss of left heart border = lingula. Loss of left hemidiaphragm = left lower lobe. 'Silhouette sign' localises the consolidation.

questions.keyPoint: CXR silhouette sign for pneumonia: Right heart border lost = right middle lobe. Right hemidiaphragm lost = right lower lobe. Left heart border lost = lingula (left upper lobe). Left hemidiaphragm lost = left lower lobe. Memorise: 'Right heart = RML, Right dia = RLL, Left heart = Lingula, Left dia = LLL'.

questions.topic: Cardiac findings on examination — identify the diagnosis (18.6 & 18.7)
questions.optionsTitle: Cardiac findings on examination — identify the diagnosis (18.6 & 18.7)
questions.stem: A newborn has a continuous (systolic AND diastolic) murmur at the left infraclavicular region with no silent period. CXR shows cardiomegaly.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: ASD: fixed split S2 is the hallmark (LA→RA shunt maintains equal RV filling regardless of respiration). ESM = increased flow across the pulmonary valve. Right heart enlargement from chronic volume overload. Close by age 3-5 if haemodynamically significant.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: PDA: continuous 'machinery' murmur because aortic-to-pulmonary pressure gradient exists throughout the cardiac cycle. Best heard at the left infraclavicular area. In premature infants: indomethacin/ibuprofen. In term child: surgical/catheter closure.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: VSD: pansystolic murmur (no ejection click) at LLSE with thrill (grade 4+). 'Maladie de Roger' = loud pansystolic murmur of a small VSD (paradox: small defect = louder, higher velocity). Most VSDs close spontaneously.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: TGA: central cyanosis from birth, NO murmur (two parallel non-mixing circuits = no turbulent flow), equal pulses in all limbs. PGE1 to keep ductus open + balloon atrial septostomy (Rashkind) + arterial switch operation.

questions.keyPoint: Innocent murmur features (STILSS): Soft (≤grade 2-3), Times with systole only (no diastolic), In a well child, Left sternal edge, Short duration, changes with position (Supine/Sitting). No radiation, no clicks, normal S2, normal ECG and CXR.

choices.label: H

questions.stem: Ji, 12-month-old girl, has fever and vomiting. She is well hydrated with coryza and inflamed pharynx. Bag urine: WBC 30/mm³, no red cells, no organisms, culture negative.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis: red cell casts are PATHOGNOMONIC of GN. 2 weeks after GAS throat infection. Haematuria + hypertension + oedema + low C3. Self-limiting; supportive treatment.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: Perineal contamination: mildly raised WBC from bag specimen = false positive (vaginal/foreskin cells wash into the bag). Clinical picture (viral URTI) makes UTI unlikely. Confirm with clean-catch or catheter specimen.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: UTI with sepsis in an infant: catheter specimen confirms UTI (no contamination). Full septic workup + IV antibiotics (after cultures + LP). NICE guideline: USS in acute illness + MCUG at 4-6 weeks for this age.

questions.keyPoint: Urine sample reliability: Bag specimen (15-30% false-positive rate) → clean catch (best for most children) → catheter specimen (reliable in sick infants) → suprapubic aspiration (gold standard). Never diagnose UTI from bag specimen alone.

choices.label: B

questions.stem: Jason, 3-week-old, is still jaundiced despite reassurance it was 'breast milk' jaundice. He now presents with poor feeding, vomiting, bruising, PALE STOOLS, and liver 4 cm below costal margin.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Biliary atresia: PALE STOOLS + conjugated jaundice + hepatomegaly = URGENT investigation. NEVER attribute pale stools to breast milk jaundice. Kasai hepatoportoenterostomy MUST be performed before 8 weeks of age for best outcome.

choices.label: F
choices.explanation: Hepatitis A: faeco-oral transmission, endemic in developing countries. Travel + diarrhoeal illness + jaundice + elevated ALT. Anti-HAV IgM confirms acute infection. Self-limiting.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Galactosaemia: vomiting + jaundice + hepatomegaly + reducing substances in urine (galactose) with ANY milk feeding (lactose-containing). Fatal without treatment. Uridyltransferase enzyme assay confirms. Galactose-free diet is curative.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Wilson disease: copper accumulation from ATP7B gene mutation. Liver disease + KF rings (copper in Descemet membrane, seen on slit-lamp) + low caeruloplasmin + raised 24h urinary copper. Treat with D-penicillamine or trientine.

questions.keyPoint: Conjugated jaundice rule: PALE STOOLS + DARK URINE + jaundice = conjugated = ALWAYS pathological = URGENT. NEVER reassure 'breast milk jaundice' without measuring conjugated bilirubin. Biliary atresia: window for Kasai = before 8 weeks of age.

questions.stem: Natalia, 4, has had two episodes of tonsillitis this month. She is tired, pale, and has bruises on her lower legs. Examination shows pallor, purpuric skin lesions, and hepatosplenomegaly.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALL: bone marrow infiltration causes anaemia (pallor), thrombocytopenia (purpura/bruising), neutropenia (recurrent infections), and hepatosplenomegaly. FBC shows anaemia + thrombocytopenia ± blast cells. Bone marrow aspirate confirms (>20% blasts).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Brain tumour with raised ICP: morning headaches + personality/behavioural change + VI nerve palsy (longest intracranial course → first to be stretched by raised ICP = 'false-localizing sign'). Urgent MRI brain.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma: leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most common presenting sign. Average age 18 months. RB1 gene (13q14). Bilateral in 40% (heritable). Urgent ophthalmology + MRI brain/orbit. Avoid CT (radiation risk).

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Wilms tumour (nephroblastoma): most common before 5 years, smooth unilateral abdominal mass found incidentally. Does NOT cross midline. WT1 gene. Investigation: USS then CT. Treatment: nephrectomy + chemotherapy.

questions.keyPoint: Abdominal masses in children: <5 years — Wilms (smooth, intrarenal, DOES NOT cross midline) vs Neuroblastoma (irregular, CROSSES midline, retroperitoneal, raised urinary VMA/HVA). Investigation: USS first in ALL cases, then CT for staging.

questions.keyPoint: Abdominal masses in children: <5 years — Wilms (smooth, intrarenal, DOES NOT cross midline) vs Neuroblastoma (irregular, CROSSES midline, retroperitoneal, raised urinary VMA/HVA). Investigation: USS first in ALL cases, then CT for staging.

questions.stem: Keith, 14, has behavioural change and declining school performance. He has morning occipital headaches and cannot deviate his left eye laterally (VI nerve palsy).

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: ALL: bone marrow infiltration causes anaemia (pallor), thrombocytopenia (purpura/bruising), neutropenia (recurrent infections), and hepatosplenomegaly. FBC shows anaemia + thrombocytopenia ± blast cells. Bone marrow aspirate confirms (>20% blasts).

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Brain tumour with raised ICP: morning headaches + personality/behavioural change + VI nerve palsy (longest intracranial course → first to be stretched by raised ICP = 'false-localizing sign'). Urgent MRI brain.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma: leukocoria (white pupillary reflex) is the most common presenting sign. Average age 18 months. RB1 gene (13q14). Bilateral in 40% (heritable). Urgent ophthalmology + MRI brain/orbit. Avoid CT (radiation risk).

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Wilms tumour (nephroblastoma): most common before 5 years, smooth unilateral abdominal mass found incidentally. Does NOT cross midline. WT1 gene. Investigation: USS then CT. Treatment: nephrectomy + chemotherapy.

questions.stem: Connor, 4, is very tired with leg pain, petechial rash, pallor, and hepatosplenomegaly. FBC: low Hb and low platelets.

choices.label: A
choices.explanation: Acute leukaemia: bone marrow aspirate confirms by demonstrating >20% blast cells. Also provides immunophenotyping and cytogenetics for prognosis and treatment stratification.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Retinoblastoma: MRI of the head delineates tumour extent within and beyond the eye. CT is avoided in children (radiation risk + RB1 germline mutation = predisposition to secondary malignancies from radiation).

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Wilms tumour: USS abdomen is the first-line investigation — confirms intrarenal origin, evaluates the contralateral kidney and IVC. Safe (no radiation), quick, and widely available. CT provides detailed staging.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Neuroblastoma: urinary VMA/HVA raised in ~95% of neuroblastomas — confirms diagnosis biochemically. MIBG scan + bone marrow sampling for staging.

questions.keyPoint: Paediatric tumour markers: AFP → hepatoblastoma, yolk sac tumour. β-hCG → germ cell tumours. Urinary VMA/HVA (catecholamines) → neuroblastoma (raised in 95%). LDH → non-specific poor prognosis marker in lymphoma/leukaemia. NSE → neuroblastoma.

questions.stem: Shlomo, 9-day-old, had a religious circumcision yesterday. The wound will not stop bleeding. He is pale and tachycardic. Blood tests: low Hb, prolonged APTT, normal PT, markedly reduced Factor VIII.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: β-Thalassaemia major: HbA2 >3.5% + elevated HbF confirm β-thalassaemia. Target cells on film. Splenomegaly from haemolysis + extramedullary haematopoiesis. Regular transfusions + chelation therapy (deferasirox).

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Haemophilia A: X-linked recessive Factor VIII deficiency. Circumcision bleeding in a neonate is a classic presentation. Prolonged APTT + normal PT + low Factor VIII confirms diagnosis. Treat with recombinant Factor VIII.

choices.label: O
choices.explanation: Sickle cell disease — splenic sequestration crisis: spleen sequesters blood → acute Hb drop below baseline. Emergency: IV fluids + urgent blood transfusion + analgesia. Repeated crises → splenectomy.

questions.keyPoint: Haemophilia A vs B: Both X-linked recessive, prolonged APTT, normal PT. Haemophilia A (80%) = Factor VIII deficiency → treat with Factor VIII. Haemophilia B (15%) = Factor IX deficiency ('Christmas disease') → treat with Factor IX. vWD = most common inherited bleeding disorder (autosomal dominant) → low VWF + prolonged bleeding time.

questions.stem: Priscilla, 16, has been anaemic since childhood. She has worsening pallor and splenomegaly. Blood film: microcytic hypochromic anaemia with target cells. HbA2 = 5%, HbF = 3%.

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: β-Thalassaemia major: HbA2 >3.5% + elevated HbF confirm β-thalassaemia. Target cells on film. Splenomegaly from haemolysis + extramedullary haematopoiesis. Regular transfusions + chelation therapy (deferasirox).

choices.label: E

Haemophilia B (15%) = Factor IX deficiency ('Christmas disease') → treat with Factor IX. vWD = most common inherited bleeding disorder (autosomal dominant) → low VWF + prolonged bleeding time.

questions.stem: A 7-month-old boy has prolonged bleeding after a heel prick. Results: prolonged APTT, normal PT, extremely low Factor VIII. His maternal uncle has a similar condition.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Von Willebrand disease type 1: prolonged bleeding time (impaired platelet plug) + normal PT/APTT + low VWF antigen. Desmopressin (DDAVP) releases stored VWF from endothelium for mild-moderate Type 1 VWD. Tranexamic acid also useful for menorrhagia.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Transient aplastic crisis (parvovirus B19): parvovirus temporarily suppresses red cell production. In a child with normal baseline Hb, the marrow will recover in 1-2 weeks (reticulocyte count will rise). No transfusion needed unless severely symptomatic.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Haemophilia A: recombinant Factor VIII replacement (preferred over plasma-derived products to minimise infection risk). X-linked pattern confirmed by maternal uncle affected.

questions.keyPoint: Parvovirus B19: Normal children = mild self-limiting aplastic crisis. Children with haemolytic anaemia (SCD, hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia) = SEVERE APLASTIC CRISIS (RBC destruction ongoing while production stops → Hb drops dramatically = emergency transfusion). In pregnancy = hydrops fetalis.

questions.topic: Child and adolescent mental health interventions (24.8)
questions.optionsTitle: Child and adolescent mental health interventions (24.8)
questions.stem: Steven, 15, is brought to ED. He states the television is putting thoughts into his head and is becoming increasingly suspicious that his neighbour is spying on him. No drugs, no medical cause.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: CFS/ME: post-viral onset, physical symptoms without organic cause, functional impairment (school absence). CBT is evidence-based alongside graded activity. School reintegration planning essential. Exclude organic causes first (TFTs, FBC, coeliac, diabetes).

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Toddler tantrums: normal developmental stage (peak 18 months-3 years). Explanation, advice, and reassurance. Consistent boundaries, ignore tantrums (no reinforcement), praise positive behaviour, maintain predictable routines.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Psychosis/schizophrenia: thought insertion + paranoid delusions require urgent psychiatric assessment. Referral to CAMHS psychiatrist is mandatory. Early intervention in psychosis improves long-term outcomes.

questions.keyPoint: CFS/ME management: CBT + Graded Exercise Therapy (GET). Key principle: complete rest → deconditioning → worse symptoms. Graded activity gradually increases tolerance. Exclude: hypothyroidism (TFTs), anaemia, coeliac, depression. School reintegration plan + education health care plan for prolonged absence.

questions.stem: Ashleigh, 5, has a non-blanching rash over her buttocks and lower legs. She is well, afebrile, CRT <2 seconds, HR 100/min.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Guttate psoriasis: 'guttate' = droplet-shaped. Classic trigger = GAS throat infection. Multiple small scaly teardrop plaques on trunk and limbs. Often resolves spontaneously (unlike chronic plaque psoriasis).

choices.label: E
choices.explanation: HSP (IgA vasculitis): palpable purpura over buttocks and lower limbs in a WELL, AFEBRILE child. Classic triad: purpuric rash + colicky abdominal pain + arthralgia. Monitor urine for 6 months (IgA nephropathy). Distinguished from meningococcal disease (systemically unwell).

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Molluscum contagiosum: pearly-white umbilicated papules from poxvirus. Common ages 2-12 years. Spreads by direct contact. Self-limiting in 6-18 months. No treatment usually required.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Scabies: intense nocturnal pruritus, characteristic distribution (web spaces, wrists, waist, buttocks). Burrows are pathognomonic. Treat ALL household contacts simultaneously with permethrin 5% cream. Treat even if asymptomatic (incubation 4-6 weeks).

questions.keyPoint: Non-blanching rash: WELL + non-blanching over buttocks/legs = HSP. UNWELL + non-blanching + fever + shock = Meningococcal sepsis (EMERGENCY → IV ceftriaxone immediately). WELL + isolated low platelets = ITP. FACIAL petechiae after coughing/vomiting = innocent (Valsalva). When in doubt about meningococcal = TREAT first.

questions.stem: Julie, 12, known type 1 DM, has Kussmaul breathing, severe dehydration (CRT 5 seconds), drowsiness, and blood glucose 28 mmol/L. She is in shock.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Fluid resuscitation with normal saline (0.9% NaCl)
choices.explanation: DKA with shock: fluid resuscitation is the IMMEDIATE PRIORITY. 10 ml/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus for shock. Insulin starts AFTER fluids. NEVER give sodium bicarbonate in DKA (cerebral oedema risk). Rehydration over 48h.

choices.label: H
choices.text: Intravenous infusion of normal saline (0.9% NaCl)
choices.explanation: Sick-day hyperglycaemia with vomiting: cannot take oral fluids, haemodynamically stable, NOT in DKA. Intravenous normal saline for rehydration. Monitor glucose and ketones closely.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Mild-moderate hypoglycaemia (conscious): fast-acting oral glucose (non-diet sugary drink, 15-20g). Follow with complex carbohydrate snack. Behavioural change/aggression is a classic hypoglycaemia symptom in young children.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: New diagnosis of diabetes without DKA: start subcutaneous insulin. Child is well and stable — no IV fluids needed. Requires structured education, carbohydrate counting, sick-day rules training.

questions.keyPoint: DKA management priority: 1. AIRWAY+BREATHING 2. SHOCK → 10 ml/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus. 3. IV fluids for deficit over 48h. 4. Start insulin 1-2h AFTER fluids. 5. Add potassium early (hypokalaemia risk from insulin). 6. NEVER give bicarbonate. 7. Monitor for cerebral oedema (worsening consciousness → IV mannitol/hypertonic saline).

choices.explanation: Sick-day hyperglycaemia with vomiting: cannot take oral fluids, haemodynamically stable, NOT in DKA. Intravenous normal saline for rehydration. Monitor glucose and ketones closely.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Mild-moderate hypoglycaemia (conscious): fast-acting oral glucose (non-diet sugary drink, 15-20g). Follow with complex carbohydrate snack. Behavioural change/aggression is a classic hypoglycaemia symptom in young children.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: New diagnosis of diabetes without DKA: start subcutaneous insulin. Child is well and stable — no IV fluids needed. Requires structured education, carbohydrate counting, sick-day rules training.

questions.keyPoint: DKA management priority: 1. AIRWAY+BREATHING 2. SHOCK → 10 ml/kg 0.9% NaCl bolus. 3. IV fluids for deficit over 48h. 4. Start insulin 1-2h AFTER fluids. 5. Add potassium early (hypokalaemia risk from insulin). 6. NEVER give bicarbonate. 7. Monitor for cerebral oedema (worsening consciousness → IV mannitol/hypertonic saline).

questions.stem: Emma, 3½, has 24-hour left thigh pain and limp. Temperature 38.9°C, HR 150/min. The left thigh is swollen, held flexed/abducted, red and warm, and she cries if moved. Paracetamol has no effect.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Fractured femur — possible NAI: spiral femoral fracture in a pre-ambulatory infant is suspicious for non-accidental injury. All fractures in pre-ambulatory infants require child protection assessment, skeletal survey, and Social Care referral.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Reactive arthritis (transient synovitis) post-enteric: following Campylobacter/Salmonella/Shigella gastroenteritis. Normal X-ray, able to weight-bear (unlike septic arthritis), mildly elevated inflammatory markers. NSAIDs + rest + Kocher criteria reassessment.

choices.label: H
choices.explanation: Septic arthritis: acute onset + fever + tachycardia + severely painful hip held in position of comfort + warmth and redness = Kocher criteria positive. Orthopaedic EMERGENCY — surgical washout + IV antibiotics to prevent avascular necrosis and joint destruction.

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Growing pains: bilateral nocturnal leg pain in otherwise well children aged 3-12 years. Complete resolution overnight. Normal examination. NOT arthritis (no morning stiffness, no joint swelling). Management: reassurance, massage, stretching, paracetamol PRN.

questions.keyPoint: Hip pain by age: Infant = DDH, septic arthritis. Toddler-6y = Transient synovitis (most common), septic arthritis. 4-8y = Perthes disease (AVN, limp without fever). 10-16y = SUFE (obese adolescent male, urgent in-situ pinning). ANY AGE = Septic arthritis. Kocher criteria: fever >38.5, non-weight-bearing, ESR >40, WCC >12,000 — 4/4 = >99% probability.

questions.stem: Emily, 3, fit and healthy, banged her head on a door at nursery. Almost immediately she went very pale and stiff, then had jerking movements of her arms and legs for 20 seconds.

choices.label: B
choices.explanation: Blue breath-holding spells (cyanotic type): triggered EXCLUSIVELY by frustration/crying. Child holds breath in expiration → blue → brief LOC → rapid recovery. NOT dangerous. Do NOT reinforce avoidance with sweets (operant conditioning worsens). Resolves spontaneously by school age.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy: brief frequent staring episodes (2-30 seconds) + eyelid flickering + no post-ictal state. Hyperventilation (blowing) is a classic provocative test. EEG: 3Hz spike-and-wave (bilaterally synchronous). Treatment: ethosuximide (first-line) or sodium valproate.

choices.label: K
choices.explanation: Reflex asystolic syncope: pain/minor injury trigger → vagal activation → cardiac asystole → cerebral hypoperfusion → immediate pallor + brief jerking. No post-ictal confusion. Rapid complete recovery. Distinguish from breath-holding (cyanotic, triggered by frustration/crying).

choices.label: L
choices.explanation: Vasovagal syncope: classic prodrome (hot, dizzy) + trigger (prolonged standing) + pallor + brief anoxic myoclonus (brief jerking during syncope). Rapid and complete recovery. NOT epilepsy.

questions.keyPoint: Epileptic seizure vs syncope vs absence: Seizure = no prodrome (or aura), cyanosis during, jerking >30s, POST-ICTAL confusion, tongue biting, incontinence. Syncope = PRODROME (hot/dizzy), precipitant, pallor, brief jerking OK, QUICK RECOVERY. Absence = <30s, no falling, hyperventilation triggers, NO post-ictal state.

questions.stem: Carlos, 13, has had 6 episodes of sudden pallor and loss of consciousness over 3 months. His father died suddenly aged 34. ECG shows a prolonged QT interval.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Cardiac arrhythmia (long QT syndrome): sudden collapse during exertion + family history of premature sudden cardiac death + prolonged QTc on ECG = LQTS until proven otherwise. Long QT → Torsades de Pointes → VF → cardiac arrest. Urgent cardiology referral, beta-blockers, avoid QT-prolonging drugs.

choices.label: D
choices.explanation: Childhood absence epilepsy: brief frequent staring episodes (2-30 seconds) + eyelid flickering + no post-ictal state. Hyperventilation (blowing) is a classic provocative test. questions.stem: Anika, 15, presents with morning vomiting, abdominal pain, fatigue, and breast tenderness. She denies unprotected sex with her mother present. She has a suprapubic mass.

choices.label: C
choices.explanation: Depression: core features — persistent low mood + anhedonia + sleep disturbance + impaired concentration + declining school performance. PHQ-A for screening. First-line: CBT (mild-moderate); fluoxetine (1st-line SSRI in adolescents) if moderate-severe; CAMHS referral if severe/treatment-resistant.

choices.label: G
choices.explanation: Somatic symptoms: frequent headaches and fatigue in an adolescent without red-flag features. 25% of teenagers report weekly headaches. Often stress-related. CBT + identification of psychosocial stressors. Exclude organic causes before this diagnosis.

choices.label: I
choices.explanation: Pregnancy: morning sickness + breast tenderness + suprapubic mass (uterus) + possible missed period. Adolescents often present with somatic symptoms. Always see adolescents ALONE — she may give different history. Pregnancy test is mandatory.

choices.label: J
choices.explanation: Brain tumour (malignancy): raised ICP signs (morning headache, vomiting) + VI nerve palsy ('false-localizing sign') + behavioural change. Urgent MRI brain. Fundoscopy for papilloedema. Do NOT delay.

questions.keyPoint: HEADSS assessment (adolescent consultation): Home, Education, Activities, Drugs, Sex/Safety, Suicide/depression. ALWAYS see the adolescent ALONE for part of the consultation (UK: Fraser guidelines for confidentiality). Non-judgmental approach is essential for disclosure of sensitive behaviours.
